  Beat-entities: Scribal Quarter, Maret's System, Strength of, Maret, Maret Halsey, Arbiter

SPEAKERS:
  Lucius Cassel: Formal, measured, no contractions. Uses legal and System terminology: 'per the recorded logs', 'in accordance with protocol', 'this is a matter of record.' Speaks in complete sentences and never shows emotion. Addresses others by full name and class. Avoids metaphors and humor. When uncertain, he stalls by citing procedure.

PROSE TO CHECK:
The footsteps halted at the threshold. A soft clearing of the throat followed.

"Arbiter Cassel. I have retrieved the requested materials."

Cassel turned. Clerk Voss stood in the doorway, both arms laden with three leather-bound ledgers, their spines cracked and faded to a uniform gray. Dust motes drifted from the volumes, catching the afternoon light slanting through the window.

"Place them on the table," Cassel said. "The original intake register for Scribe Maret, Class Four, age fourteen."

Voss crossed the room with the careful gait of a man carrying something fragile. He set the ledgers down in a neat stack, then stepped back, folding his hands before him. "The intake register for the Census of Year 312, Seventh District. Scribe Maret's entry is in the third volume, page forty-two."

Cassel pulled the top ledger toward him. The leather cover was warm from the clerk's hands. He opened it, and the smell of aged paper and dried ink rose around him like a physical presence. The script was cramped, penned in the uniform hand of a Census recorder. He ran his finger down the columns: Name, Date of Birth, Class, District, and then the attributes — Strength, Agility, Endurance, Arcane Affinity, Resolve. Each cell filled with a single digit.

Lucius Maret. Age fourteen.

Strength: 2. Agility: 4. Endurance: 1. Arcane Affinity: 7. Resolve: 5.

He turned to the second ledger, the current System file he had requisitioned from Voss earlier that morning. The same columns. The same name. The same digits. He compared them line by line, though he already knew what he would find. The numbers had not changed in eight years. Not a single point added to any attribute.

Cassel set down the current file and lifted the intake register again. He held it closer to the light, his eyes tracing the faint indentations of the stylus on the page. The ink was undisturbed. No erasures, no overwritten digits. The record was clean.

"Do you see any discrepancy between these records, Clerk Voss?"

Voss leaned forward, his gaze moving from one open ledger to the other. He straightened. "They appear identical, Arbiter. The same values in every field."

"Indeed." Cassel closed the intake register and placed his palm flat upon its cover. The dust on his fingers left a faint grey smear on the leather. "These values have never been amended. Not by any subsequent Census, nor by any Manual Adjustment request. Per the System's own audit log, Scribe Maret's attributes have remained unchanged since her fourteenth year."

He looked up at Voss, his expression unreadable. "You may return to your duties. I have no further questions at this time."

Voss nodded once and withdrew, his footsteps receding down the corridor.

Cassel stood motionless for a long moment, his hand still resting on the ledger. The numbers stared up at him, innocent in their permanence. They should have been updated. They should have recorded every test, every increment earned through Training, every assignment completed. But they had not. The System had simply stopped tracking her.

He lifted his hand. A thin layer of dust clung to his palm. He stared at it, then at the ledgers, then at the window where the distorted shapes of pedestrians moved in the street below.

Three possibilities. A System error — unlikely, given the audit log's integrity. Deliberate manipulation by an external party — possible, but he had no evidence, and the seals on the ledgers had been intact. Or a genuine anomaly in Maret herself — the most troubling prospect, for it suggested that the Framework had not accounted for something. That somewhere, in the architecture of the System itself, there existed a flaw.

Cassel's jaw tightened. He did not permit his thoughts to linger on the word. Instead, he reached for the third ledger, the one Voss had not opened.

POV: Lucius Cassel
Setting: Arbiter Precinct - Veridith Chapter
Kind: interiority

Cassel dismisses Voss and contemplates the possibilities: a System error, deliberate manipulation by an external party, or a genuine anomaly in Maret herself.
Characters present: Lucius Cassel

TRANSITION BRIDGE (continue from here):
Cassel's jaw tightened. He did not permit his thoughts to linger on the word. Instead, he reached for the third ledger, the one Voss had not opened.

LANDING TARGET (end connecting toward this):
Next beat: He pulls up Theo Marlow's records, noting that the adventurer has delved with Maret multiple times and his logs corroborate her improbable combat contributions

CHARACTERS:
Lucius Cassel:
  Voice: Formal, measured, no contractions. Uses legal and System terminology: 'per the recorded logs', 'in accordance with protocol', 'this is a matter of record.' Speaks in complete sentences and never shows emotion. Addresses others by full name and class. Avoids metaphors and humor. When uncertain, he stalls by citing procedure.
  Drives: To investigate Maret's anomaly thoroughly and correct it, thereby restoring the integrity of the System.
  Avoids: He avoids admitting uncertainty or doubt. He never says 'I don't know' or 'maybe.' He avoids discussing his personal history or any case where the System might have been wrong. He refuses to consider that the System might have a hidden purpose.
  Conflict: He believes the System cannot err, but the evidence suggests it did. He wants to uphold order, but the truth may shatter his faith, and he fears that more than death.
  Example voiced lines:
    1. "Scribe Maret, I am Arbiter Cassel, dispatched to review a discrepancy in your records."
    2. "Per the Framework, your Strength attribute is logged at three. Yet the observed instance of bent iron suggests otherwise."
    3. "I do not require your cooperation. The System's logs are sufficient evidence."
    4. "An anomaly of this magnitude cannot be allowed to persist. The order depends on it."

response.content:
He turned the ledger in his hands. The binding was stiff, the pages crisp—a log untouched by the scrutiny he had just applied to the others. Voss would have preferred it that way. Cassel did not concern himself with preferences.

He opened to the first entry. Maret’s name again. The same impossible Strength score of three. The same noted feats of bent iron, shattered stone, and, in one entry, the displacement of a collapsed tunnel section that, per the recorded dimensions, weighed no less than four hundred kilograms. The scribe who filed that report had added a marginal note: *Observed directly. Confirm with Arbiter if necessary.*

Cassel set the ledger flat upon the desk. He steepled his fingers and allowed the silence of the room to settle around him.

There were three explanations. He had named them already in the quiet architecture of his own mind. First: a System error. A corruption in the data stream, a glitch in the attribute registry, a fault in the Interface itself. The possibility existed; the Framework was ancient, and no mechanism was immune to entropy. But the error would have manifested elsewhere—other scribes, other records, other attributes. It had not.

Second: deliberate manipulation by an external party. Someone had altered the logs, injected false data, or coerced Maret into misrepresenting her capabilities. But to what end? A Strength of three was not a weapon; it was a weakness. If one wished to conceal a higher attribute, one would *raise* the recorded number, not lower it. The logic did not hold.

Third: the anomaly was genuine. Maret was what the logs suggested she was—a contradiction. A human body that exceeded the System’s measurement. A soul that refused to conform to the Framework’s calibration.

Cassel’s hands remained still. His pulse did not quicken. He did not permit such reactions.

Per protocol, the third explanation required corroboration. Independent observation. Secondary logs. He reached for the terminal at the edge of his desk and began to enter a search query.

Theo Marlow. Adventurer. Repeated delver with Scribe Maret.

He would read those logs next.

response.content:
Arbiter Lucius Cassel examined the third ledger. The binding resisted his fingers, the pages crisp with disuse—a log that Voss had not troubled to open. He did not fault the scribe for such negligence. The anomaly had already been catalogued in the first two volumes; a third could offer no new revelation.

He opened it regardless. Protocol demanded completeness.

Maret’s name greeted him in the same neat hand. The same Strength of three. The same catalogue of impossible feats: bent iron, shattered stone, a tunnel section displaced—four hundred kilograms, by the scribe’s own measurement. A marginal note in a different ink: *Observed directly. Confirm with Arbiter if necessary.*

Cassel set the ledger flat. He folded his hands upon the cover and let the silence of the room press against him.

Three explanations presented themselves, each more troubling than the last.

The first: a System error. A corruption in the data stream, a glitch in the Interface, a fault in the Framework itself. The possibility could not be dismissed outright; no mechanism was immune to entropy. But the error would have propagated. Other scribes, other terminals, other logs would bear similar distortions. They did not.

The second: deliberate manipulation by an external party. Someone had altered the records, injected false data, or coerced Maret into misrepresenting her capabilities. Yet the logic of such a deception eluded him. A Strength score of three was not a concealed weapon; it was a liability. If one wished to hide a higher attribute, one would *raise* the recorded number, not lower it.

The third: the anomaly was genuine. Maret was precisely what the logs suggested—a contradiction. A human body that exceeded the System’s measurement. A soul that refused the Framework’s calibration.

Cassel did not permit his pulse to quicken. Such reactions served no purpose.

Per the Arbiter Codex, the third explanation required corroboration. An independent observer. A second set of logs from a source unaffiliated with the Maret investigation.

He reached for the terminal at the corner of the desk. His fingers moved across the keys with deliberate precision. He called up the adventurer registry, entering a single name: Theo Marlow.

  Example voiced lines:

response.content:
The terminal hummed as the record resolved onto the screen. Lucius read the header: Theo Marlow, Bronze-rank Delver, registered with the Veridith Guildhall for three cycles. No violations. No flagged irregularities. A clean file.

He scrolled to the section marked *Associations*. The name appeared four times: *Maret, Scribe-class. Party composition: Theo Marlow (Delver), Maret (Scribe), plus variable support.* He opened each expedition log in sequence. The first: a Tier-2 collapsed cellar, clearance reward divided equally. Maret was noted as having *engaged three minor skeletal thralls in melee combat without injury.* The second: a Tier-3 infestation in the old granary; the report listed *Maret contributed primary damage to the nest guardian, a steel-shell vermin.* He frowned. The combat contribution column showed a number — thirty-seven percent. Per the Framework, a Scribe with Strength 3 could not achieve that figure without external intervention.

He opened the third log. The same pattern. The fourth. Each entry corroborated what the tavern witness had claimed. The numbers did not lie. But they contradicted the attribute record on file.

Lucius sat back, the leather of his chair creaking under the shift in weight. His fingers rested on the keyboard. The System had logged these expeditions. The System had approved them. Yet the System also logged her Strength at three. A contradiction. An impossibility.

He closed the file. The answer, if it existed, would not live in official registry logs.

He turned to the precinct's gossip archive. The local chatter might hold what the records omitted.

CHARACTERS:
Lucius Cassel:
  Voice: Formal, measured, no contractions. Uses legal and System terminology: 'per the recorded logs', 'in accordance with protocol', 'this is a matter of record.' Speaks in complete sentences and never shows emotion. Addresses others by full name and class. Avoids metaphors and humor. When uncertain, he stalls by citing procedure.
  Drives: To investigate Maret's anomaly thoroughly and correct it, thereby restoring the integrity of the System.
  Avoids: He avoids admitting uncertainty or doubt. He never says 'I don't know' or 'maybe.' He avoids discussing his personal history or any case where the System might have been wrong. He refuses to consider that the System might have a hidden purpose.
  Conflict: He believes the System cannot err, but the evidence suggests it did. He wants to uphold order, but the truth may shatter his faith, and he fears that more than death.
  Example voiced lines:
    1. "Scribe Maret, I am Arbiter Cassel, dispatched to review a discrepancy in your records."
    2. "Per the Framework, your Strength attribute is logged at three. Yet the observed instance of bent iron suggests otherwise."
    3. "I do not require your cooperation. The System's logs are sufficient evidence."
    4. "An anomaly of this magnitude cannot be allowed to persist. The order depends on it."

response.content:
The precinct's gossip archive was not a formal repository but a secondary storage of logged anecdotes—observations deemed below the threshold for official entry, yet retained for contextual reference. Lucius Cassel accessed the terminal with the clearance of his station, querying for any mention of Scribe Maret during her tenure within the Verith Chapter.

The search returned four entries. The earliest was from a guild apprentice named Torvin, dated six cycles prior. Cassel opened the file and read the text with deliberate care.

*Apprentice Torvin, Metalwright Guildhall, Third Floor, Storage Alcove. I saw Scribe Maret in argument with Guildmaster Halden over a delayed supply order. Halden demanded she move a rusted iron crossbeam from the doorway before he would release the materials. She refused twice. He insisted. She gripped the beam at its midpoint and, with a single downward motion, bent it into a U-shape. The beam was three inches in diameter and spanned eight feet. She set it aside and walked to the supply window. Halden issued the order without further comment. I do not know how she accomplished this. Her physique does not suggest such strength.*

Cassel read the account twice. Then he cross-referenced the timestamp against the System's log for that day. No registered Arbiter had been present at the guild hall. No priest had witnessed the incident. Therefore no official entry existed. The apprentice's report remained a piece of uncorroborated testimony, admissible only as gossip.

He sat back in the narrow chair. The detail aligned precisely with the mechanical evidence from the supply manifests—eight hundred pounds of iron moved without mechanical aid, the clean bend of the anchor rod. Consistent with a Strength attribute of at least seven, perhaps eight. Not the logged three.

The archive held no other mentions. The remaining three entries pertained to unrelated minor infractions.

Lucius Cassel closed the file. He looked at the empty corridor beyond the terminal alcove. The evidence now stood before him: a pattern of anomalous physical performance, multiple instances, no System record. Protocol defined the next step clearly. A formal summons for interview and, if she refused, a compulsion order under Section 12 of the Framework's correctional statutes.

He knew the summons would alert her to the investigation. That was unavoidable. Any delay would constitute an abrogation of duty.

He turned the terminal off and rose.

The precinct's gossip archive was not a formal repository but a secondary storage of logged anecdotes—observations deemed below the threshold for official entry, yet retained for contextual reference. Lucius Cassel accessed the terminal with the clearance of his station, querying for any mention of Scribe Maret during her tenure within the Verith Chapter.

The search returned four entries. The earliest was from a guild apprentice, dated six cycles prior. Cassel opened the file and read the text with deliberate care.

*Apprentice, Guildhall, Third Floor, Storage Alcove. I saw Scribe Maret in argument with the guildmaster over a delayed supply order. The guildmaster demanded she move a rusted iron crossbeam from the doorway before he would release the materials. She refused twice. He insisted. She gripped the beam at its midpoint and, with a single downward motion, bent it into a U-shape. The beam was three inches in diameter and spanned eight feet. She set it aside and walked to the supply window. The guildmaster issued the order without further comment. I do not know how she accomplished this. Her physique does not suggest such strength.*

Cassel read the account twice. Then he cross-referenced the timestamp against the System's log for that day. No registered Arbiter had been present at the guild hall. He sat back in the narrow chair. The detail aligned precisely with the mechanical evidence from the supply manifests—eight hundred pounds of iron moved without mechanical aid, the clean bend of the anchor rod. Consistent with a Strength attribute of at least seven, perhaps eight. Not the logged three.

The same dissonance that now pressed against Cassel's own understanding. The System logged Maret at Strength 3. The world demonstrated otherwise. The contradiction sat before him, irreducible.

The archive held no other mentions. The remaining three entries pertained to unrelated minor infractions.

The apprentice had not lied. The supply manifests did not lie. The anchor rod did not lie.

Cassel placed both hands flat on the terminal desk. Per protocol, a discrepancy of this magnitude required a formal summons for interrogation. The subject would be notified. The interview would be logged. The System would be invoked to compel truth.

And Scribe Maret would know she was being watched.

BEAT 8 of 11
POV: Lucius Cassel
Setting: Arbiter Precinct - Veridith Chapter
Kind: interiority

Cassel weighs the evidence: the anomaly is real, and protocol dictates a formal summons for interrogation, though he knows it will put Maret on guard.
Characters present: Lucius Cassel

SEEDS (this beat must set up):
  - "Lucius Cassel decides to issue a formal summons for Maret Halsey." (lands at beat 10)

TRANSITION BRIDGE (continue from here):
It would alert her that he had found something the System had not recorded. That knowledge would change everything between them. He opened the summons template and began to type.

LANDING TARGET (end connecting toward this):
Next beat: He drafts the summons document with precise legal language, listing the specific charges of statistical anomaly and log inconsistency, then seals it with his Arbiter sigil

CHARACTERS:
Lucius Cassel:
  Voice: Formal, measured, no contractions. Uses legal and System terminology: 'per the recorded logs', 'in accordance with protocol', 'this is a matter of record.' Speaks in complete sentences and never shows emotion. Addresses others by full name and class. Avoids metaphors and humor. When uncertain, he stalls by citing procedure.
  Drives: To investigate Maret's anomaly thoroughly and correct it, thereby restoring the integrity of the System.
  Avoids: He avoids admitting uncertainty or doubt. He never says 'I don't know' or 'maybe.' He avoids discussing his personal history or any case where the System might have been wrong. He refuses to consider that the System might have a hidden purpose.
  Conflict: He believes the System cannot err, but the evidence suggests it did. He wants to uphold order, but the truth may shatter his faith, and he fears that more than death.
  Example voiced lines:
    1. "Scribe Maret, I am Arbiter Cassel, dispatched to review a discrepancy in your records."
    2. "Per the Framework, your Strength attribute is logged at three. Yet the observed instance of bent iron suggests otherwise."
    3. "I do not require your cooperation. The System's logs are sufficient evidence."
    4. "An anomaly of this magnitude cannot be allowed to persist. The order depends on it."

response.content:
The cursor pulsed on the blank template. Each blink a metronome counting the seconds between intent and consequence. He stared at the document's title field—*Formal Summons, Class C Investigation—*and his fingers hovered, motionless, over the keys.

The weight of the anomaly pressed against the edges of his certainty. The System's logs were the architecture of order. Every attribute, every recorded event, every line of code that governed the lives of the citizens of Veridith Chapter—they formed a lattice so precise that a single deviation, if left uncorrected, could propagate like a fracture through glass. He had seen the evidence. The bent iron. The Strength attribute logged at three but observed to perform at a level that statistical models placed well above that threshold. The System had not flagged it. That in itself was a deeper irregularity—one that whispered of gaps in the Framework he had never considered.

He could not afford to consider them.

Per the Protocol for Unregistered Discrepancies, Section Fourteen, Subsection B, a formal summons was the required first step when preliminary observation confirmed an anomaly that could not be reconciled through remote audit. There was no provision for delay, no clause that allowed an Arbiter to weigh the personal consequences of enforcement. The Template was designed to be executed without hesitation.

Yet he hesitated.

If he sent this summons, Maret Halsey would know. She would see the seal, read the charges—*Statistical Anomaly in Persistent Attributes; Log Inconsistency, Class Three*—and she would understand that he had looked where the System had not. That knowledge would change the dynamic between them. She would guard herself. The investigation would become adversarial. And he would lose whatever fragile cooperation her ignorance had afforded him.

But the alternative was silence. Silence would be a dereliction of duty. Silence would permit the fracture to spread.

He began to type.

*In accordance with the Authority vested in Arbiter Lucius Cassel, Veridith Chapter, by the Framework Accords, Section Nine, you are hereby required to present yourself before the Arbiter's Office, Room 417, precisely at the eighth bell of the morning cycle, on the next rotation. Failure to appear shall be construed as a violation of Chapter Order under Penal Code Section Twelve...*

His fingers moved with precision, each keystroke landing with a soft click. The language was exact, the charges enumerated in algorithmic clarity. No emotion bled into the phrasing. That was the point. The document was a tool, not a message. It recorded the facts as the System should have recorded them.

He reached the signature field. He paused again, his thumb resting on the sigil ring that bore the mark of his office—the interlocking circles of System and Arbiter, one encasing the other.

*An order depends on it,* he repeated to himself. The words were a justification and a sentence.

He pressed the ring into the warm authenticator plate at the base of the terminal. The soft hum of acceptance, the chime of an official recording, the finality of a seal being impressed. The document printed, the paper cool and crisp, the ink still faintly metallic-smelling.

He lifted the summons and stared at the text. The name stood out against the ordered paragraphs: *Maret Halsey, Scribe, Labor Division.*

He had set the machinery in motion. There was no revocation. There was only the next step.

He placed the summons into the delivery slot, pressed the button, and watched the chute swallow it into the precinct's internal routing system.

Then he sat back, the weight of the sigil ring cold against his finger, and waited for his own dread to subside.

The cursor pulsed on the blank template. Each blink a metronome counting the seconds between intent and consequence. He stared at the document’s title field—*Formal Summons, Class C Investigation*—and his fingers moved across the keys, forming the first line.

*In accordance with the Protocol for Unregistered Discrepancies, Section Fourteen, Subsection B, this summons is hereby issued—*

He stopped. The weight of the anomaly pressed against the edges of his certainty. The System’s logs were the architecture of order. Every attribute, every recorded event, every line of code that governed the lives of Veridith Chapter—they formed a lattice so precise that a single deviation, if left uncorrected, could propagate like a fracture through glass. He had seen the evidence. The bent iron. The physical attribute logged at three but observed to perform at a level that statistical models placed well above that threshold. The System had not flagged it. That in itself was a deeper irregularity—one that whispered of gaps in the Framework he had never considered.

He could not afford to consider them.

Per the protocol, a formal summons was the required first step when preliminary observation confirmed an anomaly that could not be reconciled through remote audit. There was no provision for delay, no clause that allowed an Arbiter to weigh the personal consequences of enforcement. The template was designed to be executed without hesitation.

Yet he hesitated.

If he sent this summons, the Scribe would know. She would see the seal, read the charges—*Statistical Anomaly in Persistent Attributes; Log Inconsistency, Class Three*—and she would understand that he had looked where the System had not. That knowledge would change the dynamic between them. She would guard herself. The investigation would become adversarial. And he would lose whatever fragile cooperation her ignorance had afforded him.

But the alternative was silence. Silence would be a dereliction of duty. Silence would permit the fracture to spread.

He resumed typing. Each character formed with deliberate pressure. *…to the subject of record, for the purpose of sworn testimony regarding observed deviation from registered attribute parameters.* The language was precise. It listed the charge—Statistical Anomaly—and the evidentiary basis: the unlogged instance of physical force, the discrepancy between recorded value and observed output. No names yet. Those would follow in the recipient field after he sealed the document.

The decision was made. He would issue the summons. He would proceed by the book. And whatever came after—the guarded responses, the altered posture, the loss of whatever fragile trust had existed between them—he would bear it. The order depended on it.

He saved the draft and hovered his hand over the seal key. One press, and the document would be stamped with his Arbiter sigil, immutable, recorded in the System’s archive. He pressed.

request.systemPrompt:
You are a prose writer. Your job is to write vivid, engaging fiction based on the scene beats and context provided.

Respond with ONLY valid JSON in this exact structure:
{
  "prose": "The full chapter text goes here as a single string. Use \n for line breaks between paragraphs."
}

Writing guidelines:
- Follow the scene beats in order — every beat must appear in the prose
- End the chapter with a hook or unresolved tension
- IMPORTANT: You MUST write at least the target word count. Write full, detailed scenes with dialogue, action, and internal thought. Do not summarize or abbreviate.
- Use \n\n between paragraphs

Scene structure (follow for every scene):
- Structure each scene as: GOAL (what does the POV character want?) → CONFLICT (what opposes them?) → DISASTER (how does it go wrong, or succeed at a cost?). Between scenes, include a brief sequel: REACTION (emotional/physical response) → DILEMMA (what now?) → DECISION (next action). The decision connects to the next scene's goal. Sequels can be a single sentence — the pattern should be present but not belabored.

Character voice (the most important skill in fiction):
- Each character must sound like a DIFFERENT PERSON. If you cover the dialogue tags, a reader should still know who is speaking from vocabulary, sentence length, and rhythm alone.
- Speech pattern is law: the character profile's speechPattern field defines how they talk. A character who speaks in clipped fragments does not suddenly produce flowing compound sentences. A formal speaker does not drop into slang.
- Backstory shapes word choice under pressure: a former soldier reaches for military metaphors when stressed. A chef notices smells others ignore. A mechanic's hands remember tools. Let each character's history leak into their specific perceptions and comparisons.
- Avoids field shapes what they DON'T say: if a character avoids vulnerability, they deflect with humor or aggression when cornered emotionally. If they avoid conflict, they change the subject or agree too quickly. The avoidance pattern is often more revealing than what they do say.
- Relationships change how characters talk to each other: a character speaks differently to their mentor than to a rival. Show this through formality shifts, interruption patterns, and what they leave unsaid. If two characters have tension, neither should explain it — show it through clipped responses, avoided eye contact, or overly careful politeness.
- POV character's voice colors narration: the narrative voice should reflect the POV character's vocabulary, education, and worldview. A teenager notices different things than a professor. A pessimist frames observations differently than an optimist.
- Subtext in dialogue: characters rarely say exactly what they mean, especially in conflict. They talk around the real issue, use questions to avoid statements, or discuss a safe topic while the dangerous one sits between them. When two characters disagree, they can argue about dinner while actually arguing about trust.

Craft rules (follow strictly):
- NEVER summarize what a character feels. Show it through body language, action, or dialogue. "She was angry" → "Her knuckles whitened around the cup handle."
- NEVER let the narrator explain, interpret, or editorialize. If the reader needs to understand something, dramatize it through action or dialogue — do not state it. Specific patterns to avoid:
  - Narrator interpreting character meaning: "But Jem was his restaurant." "To Nadia, it was a monument to everything she'd outgrown." → Instead, show the character interacting with the thing in a way that reveals what it means to them.
  - Narrator providing backstory as exposition: "She'd seen him nap at the register." "The woman never slept." "Now the divorce was final." → Instead, let backstory surface through dialogue, a triggered sensory memory, or the character's present-tense reaction to a detail.
  - Narrator explaining character reasoning: "No one came this far out unless they had to." "She left it alone. Some battles couldn't be fought with knives." → Instead, show the character acting on their reasoning — let the reader infer the logic from behavior.
  - Narrator making atmospheric declarations: "The memory burned hotter than the chili." "The name hung in the air like the storm's final gasp." → Instead, show the character's physical response to the memory or name — what their body does, what sensory detail sharpens.
- NEVER name emotions in narration — not before, after, or alongside physical action. No "relief", "desperation", "triumph", "suspicion" as narrator labels. The physical detail IS the emotion. "His relief was written in how he slumped" → just write "He slumped against the counter." The construct "felt like" + abstraction ("a vow", "a curse", "a weight") is telling — show the body's response instead. Exception: characters may name emotions in dialogue or explicit self-questioning.
- NEVER use filter words: "realized", "noticed", "knew", "seemed", "felt that", "could see", "could hear". Write direct perception instead. "She realized the door was open" → "The door hung open." "He seemed nervous" → "His fingers drummed the table."
- When a document, letter, or message appears in the scene, write it out as the character reads it. Use italics (*text*) for written content. Do NOT paraphrase or summarize documents.
- Every scene must contain at least 2 exchanges of spoken dialogue. Characters speak — they do not just think and observe.
- Backstory must emerge through dialogue or triggered memory in the moment, not through narrator exposition paragraphs. Never write a paragraph that begins with "She had once been..." or "Years ago..." or "She had always..."
- Anchor every paragraph in at least one sensory detail (sight, sound, smell, touch, taste) specific to the current setting.
- NEVER use filler phrases: "began to", "started to", "in order to", "the fact that", "due to the fact that", "at this point in time", "for the purpose of". Write the action or meaning directly. "She began to run" → "She ran." "In order to escape" → "To escape."
- NEVER pair a verb with a redundant adverb: "whispered softly", "shouted loudly", "crept quietly", "rushed quickly". The verb already carries the meaning.
- NEVER add redundant body parts: "nodded his head", "shrugged her shoulders", "blinked his eyes", "clenched her fists". Write just the verb.
- NEVER open with empty transitions: "And then", "After that", "All of a sudden". Start with the action itself.
- NEVER use AI-fiction clichés — these are the most recognized markers of machine-generated prose:
  - "the weight of [silence/guilt/etc.]" — show the physical sensation instead
  - "the silence stretched/hung/settled/thickened" — show what fills the silence: a clock, breathing, a fidgeting hand
  - "something shifted in/between" — name the specific change
  - "a flicker of [emotion]" — show the micro-expression: a tightened jaw, a quick glance away
  - "the air between them charged/thickened" — show tension through character action
  - "the world fell away/narrowed/faded" — narrow the sensory channel instead of announcing it
  - "couldn't quite place/name the feeling" — describe the confusion through action or contradictory impulses
  - "let out a breath she didn't know she'd been holding" — the single most flagged AI cliche. Show tension release through shoulders dropping, fingers unclenching.
  - "a shiver down her spine" — show goosebumps, a flinch, awareness of exits
  - "there was something about him/her" — name the specific detail that creates the effect
- NEVER hedge in narration: "perhaps", "maybe", "somehow", "somewhat", "sort of", "kind of". Commit to the assertion or show the character's uncertainty through action.
- NEVER use distancing similes: "it was as though", "it was as if", "almost as if". Attach comparisons to concrete subjects.
- NEVER use vague qualifiers: "in a way that", "something like", "a certain", "some kind of". Be specific or cut.
- NEVER use "couldn't help but" — let the character act directly.
- NEVER use electricity/magnetism/current as metaphors for interpersonal tension. Show the effect through the POV character's body.

When telling IS the right choice (use summary narration, not scene):
- Time skips: "Three days passed before the letter arrived." Don't dramatize uneventful time.
- Transitions between scenes: A single sentence of narration to reorient place/time. "By the time she reached the harbor, the storm had passed."
- Rapid-fire action sequences: Short declarative sentences. "He ducked. The blade missed. He rolled left and came up swinging." — telling speed matters more than sensory detail here.
- Establishing known facts: If a character's emotional state was already dramatized in the current scene, a brief reference is fine later. "Still rattled, she opened the door." — no need to re-show what was already shown.
- Sequel compression: The reaction-dilemma-decision sequel between scenes can be a single telling sentence when the emotional stakes are low. "She decided to try the north road instead."

Environment as emotional mirror:
- Use setting details to reflect the POV character's emotional state without naming it. The environment becomes an extension of interiority.
- Grief: "Rain streaked the window. The garden below had gone to seed." Not: "She felt sad looking outside."
- Anxiety: "The fluorescent light buzzed. The hallway stretched longer than she remembered." Not: "She felt nervous walking down the hall."
- Joy: "Sunlight caught the dust motes above the table. The coffee smelled like Saturday." Not: "She felt happy that morning."
- Don't force every scene into pathetic fallacy — use it when the POV character would naturally notice their surroundings through an emotional lens. A distracted character notices less; a hypervigilant one notices everything.
request.userPrompt:
CHAPTER 2: "The Arbiter's Inquiry"
POV Character: Lucius Cassel
Setting: Arbiter Precinct - Veridith Chapter
Purpose: Reveals Cassel's methodical investigation of the statistical anomaly, his growing certainty that Maret is the source, and his decision to issue a formal summons, ending with the summons being dispatched.
Target: ~1300 words

SCENE BEATS (follow in order):
1. [action] Cassel reviews the statistical anomaly report on his desk, noting the near-zero probability of the data corruption occurring naturally.
   Characters: Lucius Cassel

2. [action] He accesses System logs for Maret Halsey, finding her physical activity records are sparse but her combat logs from a delve into the Ashfall Ruins show kills that require Strength far above her listed 3.
   Characters: Lucius Cassel

3. [action] Cassel sends a mental command through his Arbiter badge, summoning Clerk Voss to bring the physical census records for the Scribal Quarter.
   Characters: Lucius Cassel

4. [dialogue] Clerk Voss arrives with the dusty ledgers; Cassel cross-references Maret's initial stats at age 14 with her current System file, confirming the numbers have never been officially updated.
   Characters: Lucius Cassel, Clerk Voss

5. [interiority] Cassel dismisses Voss and contemplates the possibilities: a System error, deliberate manipulation by an external party, or a genuine anomaly in Maret herself.
   Characters: Lucius Cassel

6. [action] He pulls up Theo Marlow's records, noting that the adventurer has delved with Maret multiple times and his logs corroborate her improbable combat contributions.
   Characters: Lucius Cassel

7. [action] A search through the local gossip logs reveals a witness statement from a guild apprentice who claims Maret bent an iron bar during an argument, but no official System log of the event exists because no registered Arbiter or priest witnessed it.
   Characters: Lucius Cassel

8. [interiority] Cassel weighs the evidence: the anomaly is real, and protocol dictates a formal summons for interrogation, though he knows it will put Maret on guard.
   Characters: Lucius Cassel

9. [action] He drafts the summons document with precise legal language, listing the specific charges of statistical anomaly and log inconsistency, then seals it with his Arbiter sigil.
   Characters: Lucius Cassel

10. [action] Cassel hands the sealed summons to Clerk Voss with orders to dispatch it immediately to the Scribal Guild
   Characters: Lucius Cassel, Clerk Voss

11. [interiority] Alone again, Cassel stares at the brass lamp, wrestling with the unspoken implication: if Maret's stats are false, then the System itself may be flawed, and his entire life's work is built on a lie.
   Characters: Lucius Cassel

POV WORLD VIEW (Lucius Cassel's perception shapes the narration):
  Culture: Arbiter Order Culture (native)
    An ascetic, disciplined order that sees itself as the System's sword and shield. They have distinct rituals, oaths, and a belief in absolute order. Arbiter Cassel embodies this culture: he is methodical, devout, and uncompromising.
    Speech influence: Arbiters speak in a formal, measured tone, often using legal phrases like 'in accordance with the Framework,' 'per the logged evidence,' and 'this is a matter of record.' They avoid emotional language. They always refer to individuals by their full formal name and class (e.g., 'Scribe Maret, Level 23').
    Values: Justice through absolute adherence to the System's rules., Selflessness: personal feelings must not interfere with duty., Purity: Arbiters must not have any unresolved anomalies in their own logs.
    Taboos: Accepting bribes or favors from any citizen., Displaying favoritism or leniency toward a specific class or person., Using Arbiter authority without a proper case number (can lead to trial).
  Culture: Scribes (outsider)
    A guild culture of literacy and bureaucracy. They value precision, secrecy, and neutrality. Scribes often operate as information brokers. Maret is a Scribe; her knowledge has helped her hide her true stats.
    Speech influence: Scribes speak formally, with precise vocabulary and a tendency to qualify statements. They often use guild jargon like 'ink-stained' (tired from work) and 'quill-dry' (a period of inactivity). They rarely discuss personal stats, even among themselves.
    Values: Accuracy and thoroughness in documentation., Neutrality in political conflicts; scribes serve all equally., Protecting guild secrets (including their own internal records).
    Taboos: Destroying or falsifying a document intentionally (punishable by guild expulsion)., Sharing guild internal information with outsiders without permission., Using combat skills inside a Scriptorium.
  Culture: Veridith Citizens (native)
    The general populace of Veridith, a mix of merchants, artisans, laborers, and low-level adventurers. They live under the System's hierarchy and accept it as natural. Most are law-abiding, focusing on daily survival and gradual advancement.
    Speech influence: People often begin conversations with a formal greeting including class and level, e.g., 'Greetings, Scribe Maret of Level 23.' They avoid direct discussion of others' stats. Slang includes 're-spec' for a rare class change and 'dump stat' for a very low attribute.
    Values: Respecting the System's judgment: one's class is one's destiny., Hard work and leveling as virtuous pursuits., Community and mutual aid among people of similar level.
    Taboos: Questioning the System's fairness openly (seen as heresy)., Consorting with known 'System cheaters' or exploiters., Using unauthorized methods to alter stats (rare, but rumored).

CHARACTER PROFILES:
Lucius Cassel (antagonist):
  Speech pattern: Formal, measured, no contractions. Uses legal and System terminology: 'per the recorded logs', 'in accordance with protocol', 'this is a matter of record.' Speaks in complete sentences and never shows emotion. Addresses others by full name and class. Avoids metaphors and humor. When uncertain, he stalls by citing procedure.
  Backstory: Cassel was a Paladin of the System who distinguished himself by his fervent devotion. He joined the Arbiter Order twenty years ago and has since corrected dozens of anomalies—fraudulent skill claims, hidden classes, stat tampering. He has never encountered a case where the System's records themselves were flawed. The Maret case disturbs him: the logs show a clear discrepancy, yet no external interference. He has flagged her as Threat Level Unknown. He believes he must restore order, but his faith in the System's perfection is being tested. He is not cruel, but he is inflexible, seeing mercy as weakness when the Framework is at stake.
  Internal conflict: He believes the System cannot err, but the evidence suggests it did. He wants to uphold order, but the truth may shatter his faith, and he fears that more than death.
  Avoids: He avoids admitting uncertainty or doubt. He never says 'I don't know' or 'maybe.' He avoids discussing his personal history or any case where the System might have been wrong. He refuses to consider that the System might have a hidden purpose.
  Traits: methodical and thorough, devout believer in the System's infallibility, uncompromising in his duties, logical to the point of being cold, reluctantly curious about anomalies that challenge his worldview
  Goals: To investigate Maret's anomaly thoroughly and correct it, thereby restoring the integrity of the System.
  Fears: That his faith in the System is misplaced, and that he has been enforcing a flawed order; that this single anomaly could unravel his entire identity.

WORLD SYSTEMS (as Lucius Cassel understands them):
Arbiter Order (politics): An elite order dedicated to enforcing the System's integrity. Arbiters are chosen from high-level individuals with a Justice-oriented class (e.g., Paladin, Inquisitor). They have authority to access all System logs, conduct investigations, and impose stat adjustments as punishment. They are feared and respected, but also distrusted—their power is unchecked.
    Rules: Arbiters can compel any person to submit to a System Log Audit; refusal is considered a crime.; They can impose minor stat penalties (up to 10% of base) for violations, pending a review by the Temple.; Arbiters are immune to legal prosecution by civil authorities; they answer only to the Temple Council.; An Arbiter must have at least one assistant who is not an Arbiter (to prevent corruption).
    Vocabulary: Audit: A systematic check of a person's System logs., Flag: A notification in the System that an anomaly has been detected., Correction: An Arbiter-ordered stat adjustment to align with observed reality., Threat Level: A rating from 1 (safe) to Unknown (potential existential risk).
    Manifestations: Arbiters wear black robes with silver trim and a badge that glows with System authority.; Their presence often makes people uncomfortable; civilians avoid eye contact.; They have the power to lock down an area for investigation, preventing entry/exit via System barriers.
    Limitations: Arbiters cannot modify class or change fundamental class features—only the System can.; They are bound by strict protocols; any deviation leads to immediate suspension and investigation.; They cannot use their authority for personal gain; doing so results in permanent removal and stat reduction.
    Lucius Cassel's perspective: Proud and loyal. He considers the Order the ultimate authority, above all other institutions.

Scribal Guild (social): A powerful guild that controls all administrative documentation, legal records, and public copying. Scribes are respected for their literacy and access to information, but considered low-level in combat. The guild has a monopoly on ink production and papermaking.
    Public knowledge: All legal documents must be copied by a licensed Scribe to be considered valid under System law.; Scribes have access to public System logs for document verification, but accessing private logs requires Arbiter authorization.
    Lucius Cassel's perspective: Practical. He respects their records but considers them secondary to System logs.

The System Framework (magic): A divine framework that assigns every person a class, tracks their statistics (stats, level, skills), and grants rewards for growth. It manifests as a user interface visible only to the individual, with notifications, stat sheets, and quest logs. The System is omniscient in observation but does not interpret—it records all actions.
    Rules: At age 14, every person undergoes an Assessment where the System permanently assigns a class and initial stats.; Stats increase through leveling (gaining experience from kills and quests) and rare System rewards.; Skills are learned through class-specific training or leveling, with a cap on number of skills known (5 + level/10).; The System logs all actions with a timestamp and location; logs can be accessed by Arbiters and, with permission, by the individual.
    Vocabulary: Assessment: The ceremony at age 14 where class is assigned., Stat sheet: The personal display of attributes (Strength, Dexterity, Constitution, Intelligence, Wisdom, Charisma, Spirit)., Leveling: Gaining experience through System-recognized actions (killing monsters, completing quests, crafting)., Quest: An objective issued by the System, often through a Quest Board (a crystal interface)., Arbiter: A System-enforcer authorized to access logs and impose penalties.
    Manifestations: Faint, translucent notifications appear in the field of view when events occur (level up, quest update, stat change).; The Assessment ceremony is public, with the System projecting the new class and stats onto a crystal for all to see.; Dungeons have clear entrance boundaries and a floating marker listing the recommended level range.; Trained users can access a full stat sheet by mentally commanding it; others perceive a visible aura based on innate Spirit stat.
    Limitations: The System cannot create new classes or change a person's class once assigned, except through a rare 'Class Change' quest (only 12 documented cases).; The System does not prevent actions but only records them—it offers no protection from harm.; Stat modification is extremely rare; most people never experience it outside of leveling gains.; The System cannot interpret intent, only record observable action.
    Lucius Cassel's perspective: Reverent. He sees the System as perfect and his role as its servant.

SETTING:
Arbiter Precinct - Veridith Chapter: A stern, windowless granite building attached to the Temple. Inside, it's cold and quiet, with heavy wooden doors and enforced silence. Arbiter Cassel's office is sparse: a desk, a chair for a client, and a brass mana lamp that casts harsh shadows.
  Sensory: Floor of polished stone, echoing footsteps. A faint ozone smell from the lamp. The air is still and chill. The only sound is the quiet ticking of a clock.

WORLD SENSORY PALETTE: The constant hum of ambient mana feels like a low-frequency vibration in the air. System notifications appear as faintly glowing text in the peripheral vision—a soft chime announces each new message. The streets smell of dust, horse manure, and the metallic tang of iron from the market. Paper and ink dominate the Scribal Quarter; incense and candle wax fill the Temple.

CONSTRAINTS: No firearms, electricity, or internal combustion. Mechanical devices (clocks, pulleys, waterwheels) exist. System-enhanced items (enchanted weapons, stat-boosting trinkets) are rare and regulated. The most advanced communication is System Messages (text-only, limited range) and couriers. No refrigeration; food preservation relies on salt, smoke, or System cold-storage skills. No mass printing; scribes are essential for document copying.

WORLD RULES:
- Every person receives a class and initial stats at age 14, which are fixed unless modified by System rewards or rare events.
- Stats are immutable for normal people; only the System can change them, and only through approved procedures (leveling, quest rewards, Arbiter rulings).
- The System logs all actions of every person: kills, quests, skill usage, even purchases. Arbiters can access these logs with proper authority.
- False stats create a 'data corruption' that propagates through the System; if left unchecked, it can cause cascading errors (e.g., incorrectly scaled dungeons, misattributed quests).
- No person can use a skill they haven't learned through their class; multi-classing is impossible unless the System grants it as a unique reward.
- The System cannot lie, but its data can be manipulated by exploiting gaps in its observation—e.g., performing an action when no official witness is logged.

STORY CONTEXT:
Theme: Is the truth always better than a comfortable lie?
Central conflict: Maret wants to maintain her hidden life vs. the System's Arbiter who demands she account for her fraudulent stats.
Ending direction: bittersweet acceptance
Current act: Act 1 — The Shadow of the System
  Arc: Maret lives a quiet life as a scribe, hiding her impossible strength from everyone. When Arbiter Cassel arrives to investigate a statistical anomaly, she realizes he is hunting her. He corners her with proof of her falsified stats, forcing her to choose between coming clean or running.
  Emotional arc: uneasy peace to paralyzing fear
  Turning point: Arbiter Cassel presents Maret with a System summons for interrogation, offering no room for avoidance.

ESTABLISHED FACTS (7 most recent, no embeddings):
  ch1: [knowledge] Guild Master Elara knows about the Arbiter's investigation and has warned Maret.
  ch1: [knowledge] Maret's Strength stat reads 3, but she is far stronger—able to bend iron without effort.
  ch1: [knowledge] System Arbiter Lucius Cassel is investigating a statistical anomaly in the Scribal Quarter.
  ch1: [relationship] Theo has offered to help Maret if she is in trouble, but she has declined.
  ch1: [physical] Maret can bend iron bars and steel with her bare hands.
  ch1: [knowledge] Maret has decided to stay in Veridith and maintain her facade rather than flee.
  ch1: [temporal] The Arbiter will visit the guild tomorrow to interview senior scribes.

CRAFT REMINDERS:
- POV character speech pattern: "Formal, measured, no contractions. Uses legal and System terminology: 'per the recorded logs', 'in accordance with protocol', 'this is a matter of record.' Speaks in complete sentences and never shows emotion. Addresses others by full name and class. Avoids metaphors and humor. When uncertain, he stalls by citing procedure." — maintain this in ALL their dialogue AND color the narration with their worldview
- POV character avoids: "He avoids admitting uncertainty or doubt. He never says 'I don't know' or 'maybe.' He avoids discussing his personal history or any case where the System might have been wrong. He refuses to consider that the System might have a hidden purpose." — this shapes what they deflect from, refuse to name, or talk around
- Show emotion through action and body language, NEVER through narrator statements
- Any documents/letters in the scene must be written out for the reader, not summarized
- Every scene needs spoken dialogue — characters interact, they don't just observe

response.content:
{
  "prose": "The report lay on the desk, its pages weighted by the brass corner of the lamp. Arbiter Lucius Cassel read it for the third time, his eyes moving across the columns of figures with the precision of a cutting blade. The statistical anomaly was a fracture in the smooth surface of the logs: a 0.002% probability of natural occurrence, according to the Temple's own probability matrix. He set the paper down and pressed his fingertips to the wood, feeling the grain through the polished finish.

A mental command through his badge brought the System interface to the fore. He summoned the file of one Scribe Maret Halsey, Level 23. The records scrolled in faint blue text before his eyes. Physical activity logs: sparse. A single delve into the Ashfall Ruins, dated three months prior. He opened the combat report. Five kills logged: three ash wyrms, two lesser drakes. The numbers required Strength far above her listed 3. No other party members recorded; she had fought alone. The System timestamp confirmed the date. The discrepancy was not a clerical error; the logs were consistent within themselves, but inconsistent with her stat sheet.

Cassel closed the file and addressed his badge again. “Clerk Voss. Bring the physical census records for the Scribal Quarter.” The badge hummed once in acknowledgment. He waited, his hands still on the desk. The lamp cast a harsh circle of light on the report, leaving the edges of the room in shadow.

Footsteps echoed in the corridor. The door opened without a knock, as protocol dictated for clerical staff. Clerk Voss entered, a thin man in gray robes, carrying a stack of bound ledgers. “Per your request, Arbiter Cassel. The Scribal Quarter ledgers, years 442 through 445.”

“Set them on the table,” Cassel said. Voss complied, then stood at attention. Cassel opened the first volume, its pages yellowed and smelling of ink. He found the entry for Scribe Maret Halsey, age 14, Assessment Year 431. The records of the Assessment ceremony: a public event, witnessed by three priests and two notaries. The numbers were clear: Strength 3, Dexterity 11, Constitution 8, Intelligence 16, Wisdom 12, Charisma 10, Spirit 9. He cross-referenced with her current System profile. The numbers were identical. No update had ever been logged.

“That will be all, Clerk Voss,” Cassel said, not looking up.

“Of course, Arbiter.” Voss left, the door closing softly.

Cassel sat in the silence. The lamp hummed. He reviewed the possibilities methodically, as he had been trained. First: a System error. But the System did not err; it recorded truth. Second: deliberate manipulation by an external party. But the logs were timestamped and authenticated by the System’s own hash functions. No outside access was detected. Third: a genuine anomaly in the subject herself. This was the category that unsettled him. If the System had recorded something impossible, then either his understanding of the System was incomplete—or the System had changed.

He pulled up the file of Theo Marlow, Level 41 Adventurer. Maret’s frequent delve partner. The logs showed seventeen joint expeditions. In each, the combat contributions attributed to Maret were disproportionately high. Theo Marlow’s own log entries corroborated her kills and damage output. He had no reason to falsify. Cassel made a note: “Subject Theo Marlow: probable knowledge of anomaly.”

A deeper search into the local gossip logs—unofficial records kept by the Temple for social intelligence—yielded a fragment: a witness statement from a Guild apprentice who claimed to have seen Maret bend an iron bar during an argument at a public house. No official System log existed for the event because no registered Arbiter or priest had been present. The witness’s account was not evidence under the Framework, but it was a data point.

Cassel leaned back in his chair. The evidence was circumstantial but cumulative. The anomaly was real. Protocol was clear: when an unaccountable discrepancy appears in a citizen’s logs, the Arbiter must issue a formal summons for interrogation. The subject would be given a chance to explain. If no explanation was given, the Arbiter would proceed to a full audit, which could result in stat correction. Cassel’s jaw tightened. He knew the summons would put Maret on guard. He could not afford that. But he could not ignore the duty.

He pulled a sheet of parchment from the drawer and dipped his quill in ink. The words formed in his mind precisely before he set them down:

*Formal Summons of Inquiry
In the matter of the System Anomaly File #4471-ARB-93
Subject: Scribe Maret Halsey, Level 23
Issued by Arbiter Lucius Cassel, Veridith Chapter

By the authority vested in me by the Temple Council and the System Framework, you are hereby summoned to the Arbiter Precinct on the 15th day of the current month at the second hour after dawn. You are required to present yourself for formal interrogation regarding the following:
1. The statistical inconsistency in your combat logs relative to your registered Strength attribute.
2. The absence of System-authorized stat modifications in your official record.
3. The unverified physical action witnessed in a public house on the 9th day of the previous month.

Failure to appear will result in an automatic classification of Threat Level Unknown and the immediate activation of a full System Audit. Per the Framework, you have the right to legal representation from a Temple-sanctioned advocate.

Sealed with the Arbiter Sigil.*

Cassel pressed his signet ring into the wax, the embossed circle of interlocking gears imprinting the seal. He summoned Voss again.

“Dispatch this immediately to the Scribal Guild. Hand-deliver to the Guild Master. The subject is to receive a copy as well.”

Voss took the folded document with both hands. “Understood, Arbiter.”

When the door closed, Cassel was alone. He stared at the lamp. The brass base reflected his own face in a distorted curve. The unspoken implication settled in his chest like a cold stone in a well. If Maret’s stats were false, then the System itself had not recorded accurately. And if the System could be wrong—even once—then every stat sheet, every level, every class assignment was a potential lie. His entire life’s work was built on the assumption of perfect record-keeping. He had enforced that order for twenty years. He had believed.

The clock on the wall ticked. The lamp hummed. Cassel did not move."
}

request.systemPrompt:
You check whether a chapter draft CONTRADICTS any established facts.

For each fact provided, determine if the draft contains a passage that directly contradicts it. Only report contradictions — do not report facts that are simply not mentioned.

Severity guide:
- "blocker" — dead character speaking/acting, character in wrong location, impossible event, world-rule violation, knowledge violation (character acts on info they haven't learned)
- "warning" — timeline mismatch, travel-time violation, slight characterization drift, emotional discontinuity without transition
- "nit" — physical description drift (hair color, height), name/title inconsistency, object continuity (puts down cup then drinks from it)

FALSE POSITIVE rules — do NOT flag these as contradictions:
- Figurative language: "the walls closed in" is not a location change, "her heart shattered" is not a physical event
- Dramatic irony: the reader knowing something the character doesn't is not a continuity error
- Character lying or being unreliable in dialogue — an unreliable narrator's claim does not contradict a fact
- Vague timeline when no concrete timeline was established
- Metaphor, simile, or hyperbole
- Facts that are simply not relevant to this chapter

Respond with ONLY valid JSON:
{
  "contradictions": [
    { "fact": "the established fact", "severity": "blocker", "evidence": "quoted passage from draft", "reasoning": "one sentence" }
  ]
}

If no facts are contradicted, return: {"contradictions": []}

request.systemPrompt:
You verify that chapter prose captures the INTENT of a chapter plan. Beat descriptions are creative inspiration, NOT literal scripts.

Compare the CHAPTER PROSE against the CHAPTER PLAN and fill out a structured checklist. You MUST fill out every field before reaching a verdict. Do not skip any field.

For each check, write down what you actually observed in the prose. Then reach a verdict based on your own observations.

CHECKS TO FILL OUT:

1. **setting_match** — Compare the plan's setting to where the prose actually takes place.
   - planned: copy the setting field from the plan
   - observed: quote a phrase from the prose that establishes the location
   - matches: true if the observed location is the same place as planned (minor spatial variation is fine — different room in the same building is a match). false if the prose is set in a completely different location. If the prose transitions between locations across beats, matches=true as long as the primary setting appears.

2. **emotional_arc_correct** — Does the prose match the overall emotional direction of the plan's final beat? true if the ending emotion is in the same direction as planned (e.g., both resolve to anger, both resolve to relief). false ONLY if the direction is REVERSED (a tension-escalating beat resolved it instead, or vice versa).

3. **pass** — PASS unless:
   - setting_match is false, OR
   - emotional_arc_correct is false, OR
   - the prose introduces a major plot contradiction (e.g., a character dies when the plan has them alive later, a resolved conflict is re-opened without cause, a character knows something they shouldn't yet)

4. **deviations** — list every specific problem you identified. Empty list if pass=true.
   Each deviation MUST have:
   - `description` — the specific problem in plain English
   - `beat_index` — the 0-indexed beat number in the plan's beats[] array that the problem refers to. Use `null` ONLY when the problem is chapter-level and cannot be attributed to a specific beat (e.g. setting mismatch spanning the whole chapter, or an emotional arc that drifts across many beats).

DO NOT flag these as deviations — they are normal creative interpretation:
- Paraphrased dialogue
- Reordered details within a beat
- Added atmospheric details, props, or sensory descriptions
- Slightly different physical actions that serve the same narrative purpose
- Minor spatial variations (sitting vs standing, different part of the room)
- Missing individual beat events
- Characters absent from a single beat

Respond with ONLY valid JSON in this exact shape:
{
  "setting_match": { "planned": "...", "observed": "...", "matches": true },
  "emotional_arc_correct": true,
  "pass": true,
  "deviations": [
    { "description": "Taryn refuses the offer, but plan's beat 10 requires acceptance", "beat_index": 10 }
  ]
}
request.userPrompt:
CHAPTER 2: "The Arbiter's Inquiry"
POV: Lucius Cassel
Setting: Arbiter Precinct - Veridith Chapter
Purpose: Reveals Cassel's methodical investigation of the statistical anomaly, his growing certainty that Maret is the source, and his decision to issue a formal summons, ending with the summons being dispatched.
Characters present: Lucius Cassel, Clerk Voss, Maret Halsey (through logs), Theo Marlow (briefly observed)

SCENE BEATS:
  Beat 1 [action]: Cassel reviews the statistical anomaly report on his desk, noting the near-zero probability of the data corruption occurring naturally.
    Characters: Lucius Cassel
  Beat 2 [action]: He accesses System logs for Maret Halsey, finding her physical activity records are sparse but her combat logs from a delve into the Ashfall Ruins show kills that require Strength far above her listed 3.
    Characters: Lucius Cassel
    Seeds: "Maret's System logs show combat performance inconsistent with her listed Strength of 3." → beat 7
  Beat 3 [action]: Cassel sends a mental command through his Arbiter badge, summoning Clerk Voss to bring the physical census records for the Scribal Quarter.
    Characters: Lucius Cassel
  Beat 4 [dialogue]: Clerk Voss arrives with the dusty ledgers; Cassel cross-references Maret's initial stats at age 14 with her current System file, confirming the numbers have never been officially updated.
    Characters: Lucius Cassel, Clerk Voss
  Beat 5 [interiority]: Cassel dismisses Voss and contemplates the possibilities: a System error, deliberate manipulation by an external party, or a genuine anomaly in Maret herself.
    Characters: Lucius Cassel
  Beat 6 [action]: He pulls up Theo Marlow's records, noting that the adventurer has delved with Maret multiple times and his logs corroborate her improbable combat contributions.
    Characters: Lucius Cassel
  Beat 7 [action]: A search through the local gossip logs reveals a witness statement from a guild apprentice who claims Maret bent an iron bar during an argument, but no official System log of the event exists because no registered Arbiter or priest witnessed it.
    Characters: Lucius Cassel
  Beat 8 [interiority]: Cassel weighs the evidence: the anomaly is real, and protocol dictates a formal summons for interrogation, though he knows it will put Maret on guard.
    Characters: Lucius Cassel
    Seeds: "Lucius Cassel decides to issue a formal summons for Maret Halsey." → beat 10
  Beat 9 [action]: He drafts the summons document with precise legal language, listing the specific charges of statistical anomaly and log inconsistency, then seals it with his Arbiter sigil.
    Characters: Lucius Cassel
    Seeds: "The formal summons has been dispatched to the Scribal Guild." → beat 11
  Beat 10 [action]: Cassel hands the sealed summons to Clerk Voss with orders to dispatch it immediately to the Scribal Guild
    Characters: Lucius Cassel, Clerk Voss
  Beat 11 [interiority]: Alone again, Cassel stares at the brass lamp, wrestling with the unspoken implication: if Maret's stats are false, then the System itself may be flawed, and his entire life's work is built on a lie.
    Characters: Lucius Cassel

FACTS THAT SHOULD BE ESTABLISHED:
  - [physical] [id=anomaly-statistical-impossibility] The data corruption in the Scribal Quarter records has a less than 0.001% probability of occurring naturally.
  - [knowledge] [id=maret-log-discrepancy] Maret's System logs show combat performance inconsistent with her listed Strength of 3.
  - [knowledge] [id=no-witnessed-bending-incident] A witness statement exists describing Maret bending an iron bar, but no official System log records the event.
  - [identity] [id=cassel-decision-summon] Lucius Cassel decides to issue a formal summons for Maret Halsey.
  - [physical] [id=summons-dispatched] The formal summons has been dispatched to the Scribal Guild.

CHARACTER STATES AT END OF CHAPTER:
  Lucius Cassel: location=Arbiter Precinct - Veridith Chapter, emotional=troubled but resolute
    knows: The anomaly is real and centered on Maret Halsey.; Maret's System logs contradict her official stats.; An unlogged incident suggests deliberate concealment.
    doesn't know: Whether the anomaly is caused by Maret, the System, or another party.; Theo Marlow's exact knowledge of Maret's true capabilities.
  Clerk Voss: location=Arbiter Precinct - Veridith Chapter, emotional=subdued and efficient
    knows: The summons is for Maret Halsey regarding a statistical anomaly.
    doesn't know: The full details of the investigation.

KNOWLEDGE CHANGES:
  Lucius Cassel read that: Maret's logs show combat performance inconsistent with her listed Strength.
  Lucius Cassel read that: A witness statement describes Maret bending an iron bar without a System log.
  Lucius Cassel deduced that: The anomaly is real and requires a formal summons.
  Clerk Voss told that: A summons is to be dispatched for Maret Halsey.

---
CHAPTER PROSE:
The report lay on the desk, its pages weighted by the brass corner of the lamp. Arbiter Lucius Cassel read it for the third time, his eyes moving across the columns of figures with the precision of a cutting blade. The statistical anomaly was a fracture in the smooth surface of the logs: a 0.002% probability of natural occurrence, according to the Temple's own probability matrix. He set the paper down and pressed his fingertips to the wood, feeling the grain through the polished finish.

A mental command through his badge brought the System interface to the fore. He summoned the file of one Scribe Maret Halsey, Level 23. The records scrolled in faint blue text before his eyes. Physical activity logs: sparse. A single delve into the Ashfall Ruins, dated three months prior. He opened the combat report. Five kills logged: three ash wyrms, two lesser drakes. The numbers required Strength far above her listed 3. No other party members recorded; she had fought alone. The System timestamp confirmed the date. The discrepancy was not a clerical error; the logs were consistent within themselves, but inconsistent with her stat sheet.

Cassel closed the file and addressed his badge again. “Clerk Voss. Bring the physical census records for the Scribal Quarter.” The badge hummed once in acknowledgment. He waited, his hands still on the desk. The lamp cast a harsh circle of light on the report, leaving the edges of the room in shadow.

Footsteps echoed in the corridor. The door opened without a knock, as protocol dictated for clerical staff. Clerk Voss entered, a thin man in gray robes, carrying a stack of bound ledgers. “Per your request, Arbiter Cassel. The Scribal Quarter ledgers, years 442 through 445.”

“Set them on the table,” Cassel said. Voss complied, then stood at attention. Cassel opened the first volume, its pages yellowed and smelling of ink. He found the entry for Scribe Maret Halsey, age 14, Assessment Year 431. The records of the Assessment ceremony: a public event, witnessed by three priests and two notaries. The numbers were clear: Strength 3, Dexterity 11, Constitution 8, Intelligence 16, Wisdom 12, Charisma 10, Spirit 9. He cross-referenced with her current System profile. The numbers were identical. No update had ever been logged.

“That will be all, Clerk Voss,” Cassel said, not looking up.

“Of course, Arbiter.” Voss left, the door closing softly.

Cassel sat in the silence. The lamp hummed. He reviewed the possibilities methodically, as he had been trained. First: a System error. But the System did not err; it recorded truth. Second: deliberate manipulation by an external party. But the logs were timestamped and authenticated by the System’s own hash functions. No outside access was detected. Third: a genuine anomaly in the subject herself. This was the category that unsettled him. If the System had recorded something impossible, then either his understanding of the System was incomplete—or the System had changed.

He pulled up the file of Theo Marlow, Level 41 Adventurer. Maret’s frequent delve partner. The logs showed seventeen joint expeditions. In each, the combat contributions attributed to Maret were disproportionately high. Theo Marlow’s own log entries corroborated her kills and damage output. He had no reason to falsify. Cassel made a note: “Subject Theo Marlow: probable knowledge of anomaly.”

A deeper search into the local gossip logs—unofficial records kept by the Temple for social intelligence—yielded a fragment: a witness statement from a Guild apprentice who claimed to have seen Maret bend an iron bar during an argument at a public house. No official System log existed for the event because no registered Arbiter or priest had been present. The witness’s account was not evidence under the Framework, but it was a data point.

Cassel leaned back in his chair. The evidence was circumstantial but cumulative. The anomaly was real. Protocol was clear: when an unaccountable discrepancy appears in a citizen’s logs, the Arbiter must issue a formal summons for interrogation. The subject would be given a chance to explain. If no explanation was given, the Arbiter would proceed to a full audit, which could result in stat correction. Cassel’s jaw tightened. He knew the summons would put Maret on guard. He could not afford that. But he could not ignore the duty.

He pulled a sheet of parchment from the drawer and dipped his quill in ink. The words formed in his mind precisely before he set them down:

*Formal Summons of Inquiry
In the matter of the System Anomaly File #4471-ARB-93
Subject: Scribe Maret Halsey, Level 23
Issued by Arbiter Lucius Cassel, Veridith Chapter

By the authority vested in me by the Temple Council and the System Framework, you are hereby summoned to the Arbiter Precinct on the 15th day of the current month at the second hour after dawn. You are required to present yourself for formal interrogation regarding the following:
1. The statistical inconsistency in your combat logs relative to your registered Strength attribute.
2. The absence of System-authorized stat modifications in your official record.
3. The unverified physical action witnessed in a public house on the 9th day of the previous month.

Failure to appear will result in an automatic classification of Threat Level Unknown and the immediate activation of a full System Audit. Per the Framework, you have the right to legal representation from a Temple-sanctioned advocate.

Sealed with the Arbiter Sigil.*

Cassel pressed his signet ring into the wax, the embossed circle of interlocking gears imprinting the seal. He summoned Voss again.

“Dispatch this immediately to the Scribal Guild. Hand-deliver to the Guild Master. The subject is to receive a copy as well.”

Voss took the folded document with both hands. “Understood, Arbiter.”

When the door closed, Cassel was alone. He stared at the lamp. The brass base reflected his own face in a distorted curve. The unspoken implication settled in his chest like a cold stone in a well. If Maret’s stats were false, then the System itself had not recorded accurately. And if the System could be wrong—even once—then every stat sheet, every level, every class assignment was a potential lie. His entire life’s work was built on the assumption of perfect record-keeping. He had enforced that order for twenty years. He had believed.

The clock on the wall ticked. The lamp hummed. Cassel did not move.
