CHAPTER PROSE:
The Pious Rest hummed with the comfort of evening trade. A fire crackled in the great hearth, casting orange light across scarred oak tables where scribes hunched over cups of mulled wine and adventurers argued the price of cured deep-glow hides. The air carried the sharp bite of roasted meat, the yeasty breath of fresh ale, and the faint sourness of sawdust ground into the floorboards by countless boots.

Maret sat with her back to the wall, a bowl of brown stew cooling between her hands. Across the table, Theo tore a heel of dark bread in half and offered one piece to her.

"The guild's latest commission," she said, accepting the bread with a nod. "The estimate lists seventeen copper-leaf specimens requiring preservation from the archive transfer. Someone clearly failed to seal their sample satchels properly on the last expedition."

Theo grinned around a mouthful of stew. "You say that like you're not secretly thrilled. Seventeen specimens means seventeen entries in the master index. That's a whole page to yourself."

"A page that no one reads." She dipped her bread into the broth. "The record's purpose is to exist, not to be admired."

"That's the most scribe thing I've ever heard you say." He washed down the bite with a pull from his tankard. "You ever think about takin' up somethin' with a bit more... movement? Not combat, obviously. But maybe survey work? Out in the field instead of in the archive?"

Maret's spoon paused halfway to her mouth. "Survey work requires a Pathfinder attunement or a minimum Agility of 14. The record shows scribes are ill-suited for field placement beyond static logistical support."

"Right. Of course." Theo's tone was light, but his eyes lingered on her a moment too long before he looked away. "Just thinkin' out loud."

The lute player in the corner shifted into a faster reel. Laughter erupted from a table of merchants near the bar, and for a moment the tavern felt full and safe and ordinary.

Then the front door scraped open.

The door swung shut behind him with a soft click that cut through the din. The lute player faltered for half a measure, then recovered.

The man in the grey coat walked straight toward their table, boots silent on the worn floorboards. He didn't scan the room, didn't glance at the bar. His eyes were fixed on Maret, and they did not waver.

He stopped a foot from their table, one hand resting on the leather folder tucked under his arm. The candlelight caught the silver brooch at his collar—a gavel crossed with a quill, the Arbiter's seal.

"Scribe Maret Halsey," he said. The words were precise, each one clipped clean. "I am Arbiter Lucius Cassel, dispatched per protocol to review a discrepancy logged in your class records."

Theo's chair scraped back. "Hey, hold on—"

But Cassel was already pulling out the empty chair across from Maret and settling into it, setting the folder flat on the table between them. He did not ask.

"Your cooperation is not required, Journeyman Marlow," Cassel said without looking at him. "But it would be efficient."

Maret's hand moved to her tankard, fingers wrapping around the cool ceramic. *A scribe's strength is in her quill.* She felt her pulse in her throat.

"Greetings, Arbiter." Her voice came out steady. "A discrepancy. That seems unlikely. I've kept my records in order."

Cassel's mouth twitched—not quite a smile. "The System's logs are the matter, not your own." He placed his palm on the folder. "And they are very clear."

Maret's fingers rested on the edge of her tankard. The clay was warm, porous. She could crush it without effort. She did not.

"Then the logs have misread something." Her voice stayed flat, administrative. "A scribe's duties are administrative. The incident involved a falling shelf and quick reflexes. There is nothing to explain."

Lucius Cassel's mouth remained perfectly still. He flipped the folder open with one precise motion. Inside lay a single sheet, cream parchment bordered by the embossed Arbiter seal—a hollow circle stamped in black wax, the ink catching the hearthlight like wet iron. He slid it across the table. "The seal renders it valid for enforcement."

She did not touch the document. She read it upside down, scanning the formal petition for attendance, the case number, the section of the Framework cited. Her name. Her class. Her assigned precinct. The date—three mornings from now.

Her throat tightened. "An interrogation."

"Formal clarification." Cassel's hands rested on either side of the folder, palms flat. "You will be asked to explain the divergence between your recorded attributes and the observed instance of—" He paused, choosing the words carefully. "—physical capacity displayed during the incident at the Guild Repository."

"The shelf fell. People react. The System reads what it reads."

"The logs indicate otherwise, Scribe Maret." His voice remained level, but something beneath it had hardened. "Per the internal record, your Strength attribute was logged at three at the time of the event. That is a fixed value. Immutable. Yet the shelf in question—a wrought-iron structure weighing approximately forty pounds—was bent at a thirty-degree angle across its left support arm."

She said nothing.

"Forty pounds of iron does not bend at thirty degrees from a falling impact. The angle suggests deliberate force applied from below. Upward. Through the center span." He tapped the document once. "The Arbiter Precinct requires your attendance to reconcile this discrepancy."

Maret kept her hands still. The tankard sat untouched. Across the room, the fire cracked and a drunk laughed at something said in jest. The sounds belonged to a different world than the one inside her chest.

"Three days," she said.

"Three mornings. You will present yourself at the Precinct foyer by the seventh bell. An examiner will be assigned to record your testimony." Cassel straightened the folder against the table's edge. "Failure to appear constitutes obstruction under Section Twelve of the Framework. The penalty is reclassification."

Reclassification. The word sat between them like a blade laid on the cloth. A scribe reclassified was a scribe unmade—reassigned to manual labor at the outskirts, stripped of guild protections, erased from the rolls.

She reached for the document. Her fingers brushed the embossed seal. "I will be there."

Cassel nodded once, a small, mechanical motion. He closed the folder and tucked it beneath his arm. "The Arbiter Precinct thanks you for your cooperation, Scribe Maret."

He turned. His boots made soft sounds against the worn floorboards as he walked toward the door.

From the bar, Theo's voice cut through the tavern noise. "Hold on. What kind of discrepancies are we talking about? And why would an Arbiter handle a scribe's paperwork?"

Lucius Cassel’s gaze shifted to Theo with the measured economy of a man who counted every motion. His fingers remained still on the tabletop.

“Journeyman Marlow.” No warmth. No greeting. “The discrepancies pertain to recorded attribute values that do not align with observed physical evidence. Specifically, a logged Strength of three and a bent iron lantern hook requiring a minimum effective Strength of twelve to deform. As to your second question: an Arbiter handles any matter where the System’s records may have been compromised, regardless of the class involved. This falls under Section Eight of the Framework Code—anomalies in class attribution.”

He let the words settle. The tavern hearth crackled. A mug thudded against oak somewhere behind Maret’s shoulder.

“I should also note,” Cassel continued, his voice dropping to a register that barely carried past their table, “that obstruction of an Arbiter’s duty carries separate penalties under Section Fourteen. Your involvement in this matter is not requested.”

Theo’s jaw tightened. He did not look away. “Just askin’ questions. That’s not obstruction. I’ve got a right to be curious when an Arbiter walks into my regular tavern and starts talkin’ about bent iron.”

“Curiosity becomes obstruction the moment it impedes a clean investigation.” Cassel’s tone did not rise. It did not need to. “I advise you to return to your companions, Journeyman. Your party remains registered at the Adventurers’ Guild. Your next delve is scheduled for three days hence. You have preparations to attend to.”

Maret watched Theo’s fingers curl around the edge of the table. Knuckles pale. He wanted to push. She could see it in the set of his shoulders, the way his jaw worked against words he hadn’t spoken yet.

He glanced at her.

She gave a tiny shake of her head. Barely a motion. *Not now. Not here.*

Theo held her gaze for a beat longer than was comfortable, then let out a breath through his nose. “Fine.” He pushed back his chair, the legs scraping against wide oak floorboards. He did not look at Cassel again. “Maret. I’ll be at the bar if you need me.”

He left.

Cassel did not watch him go. Instead, his hand moved to the leather satchel at his side, opened the brass clasp with a soft click, and withdrew a single sheet of parchment. He placed it flat on the table between them, turned so the words faced Maret.

“This is the official summons,” he said. “Per protocol, you are to present yourself at the Arbiter Precinct for a formal review of the discrepancy. The time and date are recorded.”

The seal at the bottom glinted—a silver embossment of the Framework’s interlocking rings. Maret’s eyes traced the lines of text, her throat dry, her fingers still pressed flat against her own thigh beneath the table.

The words blurred once, then settled. Maret read them again, this time letting her eyes linger on each syllable.

*Noon. Tomorrow. Arbiter Precinct.*

She committed it to memory—the curl of the *n* in *noon*, the sharp downward stroke of the *p* in *Precinct*. A scribe's habit. Useful when the hands shook and the mind needed something solid to hold.

The next sentence she already knew. She had transcribed enough summonses to recognize the formula. *Failure to appear before the appointed Arbiter shall constitute de jure admission of culpability.*

No exceptions. No delays written into the text.

Her fingertip traced the embossed rings a second time. Cold. Precise. The Framework's mark of authority pressed into the fiber of the page, permanent as a scar.

She set the parchment down and watched it settle against the grain of the table. Outside the tavern's common room, a chair scraped stone. Someone called for another round. The sounds reached her from a great distance, as though she were underwater.

Eight years. Eight years of logging unremarkable stats, taking unremarkable commissions, living an unremarkable life in a room above a baker's shop with a window that faced an alley and never, ever drew attention. And now this.

The summons didn't accuse. It didn't need to. It was a procedural invitation—that was the fiction they maintained. *Review of discrepancy.* A neutral phrase. A polite clearing of the throat.

She read the line a third time. *Formal review.* There was nothing neutral about that.

Her hand moved to the edge of the table. The wood was warm where the candle had sat. She pressed her palm flat.

There was the guild commission. The lineage charter—three generations of births, marriages, and deaths to verify against the municipal register. Due by week's end. Critical, the guildmaster had said. The family needed it sealed before the inheritance hearing.

If she cited guild protocol, if she demanded a delay on grounds of an active contractual obligation, she could buy herself time. A day. Maybe two.

She could use that time to run.

Or she could use it to prepare.

Maret picked up the summons, folded it once along its spine, and slid it into her sleeve without looking away from the table.

The summons lay warm against her sleeve, a weight she could not ignore. Maret kept her hand still, palm flat on the table’s worn grain.

“There is a commission due before dawn,” she said. “The Scribal Guild holds a binding for the northern trade routes. Seals, manifests, route charters. Sixty-seven pages requiring attestation. If I do not complete it, the merchant convoy loses its departure window.”

Lucius Cassel did not move. His hands remained clasped behind his back, the posture of a man who had heard every excuse the city could offer. “Per Article Twelve of the Veridith Accord, an Arbiter summons supersedes guild obligation. The timetable is not negotiable, Scribe Halsey. You will present yourself at the Precinct by the seventh bell, or I am authorized to record non-compliance.”

The tavern’s murmur thinned. A spoon clinked against a bowl somewhere behind her. Maret felt the weight of eyes—the tavernkeep’s from behind the bar, a pair of traders near the door, the old woman by the hearth who had not looked up from her stew until now.

She kept her breathing even. “The Guild Master will file a formal protest. Lost revenue damages the Quarter’s reputation.”

“File it. The Framework does not accept delays.”

Cassel’s tone carried no edge, no heat. That was worse. He spoke as though reading from an ordinance she had never bothered to memorize. As though the matter had already been decided.

“I will also note,” he added, “that failure to appear—or an attempt to absent yourself from the city—will result in an immediate warrant for arrest. All gate guards and patrols will be notified before the hour is out. Veridith has two thousand three hundred registered citizens. You will not slip.”

Maret’s fingers found the folded paper in her sleeve. She pressed her thumb against its crease.

“I understand the terms, Arbiter Cassel.”

She did not say she would comply. Only that she understood.

She pressed her thumb against its crease. “I understand the terms, Arbiter Cassel.”

She did not say she would comply. Only that she understood.

Arbiter Cassel did not move to take the paper back. He let it sit between them, a dead thing on the stained oak. The tavern’s fire popped and settled, and somewhere behind the bar a glass clinked against a bottle. The air smelled of old ale and beeswax.

“Then you also understand the consequence of refusal,” he said. His voice had no edge — it was flat, procedural, as if he were reading from a statute. “If you fail to appear before the System tribunal by dawn, I will file a writ of compulsion. The warrant will be circulated to every city watch post in Veridith. Your description, your class, your last known location. Per the Framework’s emergency protocols, a fugitive marked as an anomaly triggers priority capture. No exceptions.”

Maret kept her hands still on the tabletop. The scarred wood had a long groove running through the grain, the memory of a knife drawn deep and hard. She traced its path with her thumb as she spoke.

“The watch has little interest in scribes.”

“They will have interest in a scribe who bends iron and fails to report.” He leaned back, just slightly, a motion that barely shifted the candle flame between them. “I do not say this to threaten, Scribe Maret. I say it because the System does not tolerate contradictions. If you flee, you prove the anomaly’s danger. You prove that your silence was deliberate. You prove that you are a threat to order.”

She heard the word he did not speak. It hung in the air between them, unsaid but unmistakable.

The fire crackled. A patron laughed too loudly at the bar. The sound felt distant, as if it came from behind a wall of glass.

Lucius Cassel stood. He was taller than her by half a head, and the candlelight carved shadows into the planes of his face. He left the paper on the table.

“You have until dawn.” He turned, his boots heavy on the floorboards, and walked toward the door.

Maret did not watch him leave. She looked at her hands on the scarred wood. At the document lying between them like a sealed fate. The lamp oil had begun to pool in its shallow dish, burning low.

She had known, in the abstract, that discovery meant a choice. But the abstract had never had weight before. It had never tasted of soot and tallow and the end of things.

Her thumb found the groove in the wood again. A path carved by someone else’s blade, someone else’s trouble, years before she ever set foot in this tavern.

She traced it once.

Then she stopped.

The fire in the common room had burned low, orange embers pulsing like a heartbeat beneath a blanket of ash. Maret sat alone at the corner table, her untouched cup of tea gone cold. The tavern's last patrons had drifted away an hour ago—a pair of tired merchants, a drunk who'd fallen asleep over his stew—and now the only sounds were the distant clatter of pots from the kitchen and the soft creak of the innkeeper's chair.

She traced the rim of the cup with a fingertip. The path she'd stopped tracing years ago had begun to glow again, brighter than she'd ever wanted to see it.

*Compliance.* The word sat in her chest like a stone. Walk into the Guild Assessment Hall, present her character sheet, let the etching-class readers parse her stats. Wait for the moment when their eyes would snag on the discrepancy—the +4 Strength that made her a contradiction, a living clerical error. They would call it an anomaly. They would record it. They would summon the guild's special assessors from the capital, the ones who studied System defects like specimens pinned to a board. Maret Halsey, once filed under "Journeyman Scribe," would become a case file. A research subject. A thing to be understood.

She had seen their reports in the guild archives. Thin, precise handwriting. Conclusions that left no room for argument.

*Flight.* The other fork. A bag thrown together in the dark, the back door of the tavern, the road south through the wilds—a stretch of untracked forest and scrubland that swallowed travelers whole when they weren't looking. No papers, no register, no trace. She could vanish into the frontier settlements, work for farmers who paid in bread and never asked for a class. She would become a ghost. No more guild dues, no more quiet evenings in the scriptorium with the smell of ink and old paper. No more Theo, who brought her pastries from the market and asked too many questions with his earnest, freckled face.

Her chest tightened at that last thought. She pressed a palm flat against the table's scarred surface until the grain bit into her skin. Eight years of careful silence, of building a life so small and ordinary that no one looked twice. Eight years of pretending the Strength stat on her sheet was a scribe's ink-smudge, a glitch, a nothing.

But she knew the truth. The +4 was real. It had always been real. And the System had given it to her for a reason she still did not understand.

The innkeeper's chair creaked again. A log shifted in the hearth, sending a brief flare of light across the room. Maret watched the shadows leap and settle.

*Compliance meant certainty:* her secret pried open, her body measured, her future written by someone else's hand. *Flight meant uncertainty:* hunger, cold, the constant weight of looking over her shoulder. But uncertainty also meant *choice*. She could still choose the direction of her own feet.

Except Theo would wake tomorrow and find her gone. No note. No explanation. He would ask Cassel, and Cassel would shrug, and eventually someone would say *she probably ran* and he would have to decide whether to believe it.

Maret picked up the cold cup of tea and set it down again without drinking.

She needed time. Time to think, to plan, to decide whether the cost of flight was higher than the cost of staying. One night. That was all she was asking for—one night to arrange her affairs, to close her ledgers, to look Theo in the eye one last time without knowing whether it was goodbye.

She rose from the table and turned toward the stairs where Cassel's room lay.

She stopped at the base of the stairs, one hand resting on the worn newel post. The wood was smooth from decades of gripping. She turned back to where Lucius Cassel still sat at the corner table, his hands flat on either side of the leather folder.

"Arbiter Cassel." Her voice carried across the near-empty tavern. A single lamp flickered on the bar, throwing long shadows across the floorboards. "I need until the morning."

Cassel did not move. The candle at his table burned steady, unmoved by any draft. "Define the term, Scribe Maret."

"To arrange my guild affairs." She kept her hand on the post—steady, patient. The wood held her weight without a creak. "Ledgers to close. Standing orders to transfer. A journeyman to inform." She paused. "One night."

He opened the folder. His eyes traced a line of text, then another. The silence stretched. Maret counted her breaths—three, four, five—before he closed it again with a precise snap.

"Until noon. Not a bell more." His voice carried no warmth, no sympathy. "The interrogation must begin by the twelfth hour. Per protocol, an absentee subject may be classified as a fugitive. I will not grant extensions."

She nodded once. "Noon."

"There is a further matter." He slid a hand into his coat and produced a folded slip of parchment. He did not offer it to her. "The city guard has been instructed to watch all exits from the Scribal Quarter. As of dusk, no one departs without recorded timestamps and destination. This is not negotiable."

Maret's fingers tightened on the newel post, then relaxed. "I understand."

Cassel rose. He collected the folder, tucked the parchment back into his coat, and straightened his collar. His eyes met hers for a moment—flat, assessing, revealing nothing. Then he turned and walked toward the back staircase without another word.

The door at the top of the stairs clicked shut. The candle flame trembled, then steadied.

Maret let out a long, slow breath and pressed her forehead against the cool wood of the post.

Cassel had already turned and walked away before the door clicked shut. The sound hung in the air—sharp, final, the period at the end of a sentence she hadn't wanted to read.

The tavern noise rushed back in like water filling a footprint. A mug clattered. Someone laughed. A chair groaned under shifting weight.

Theo's hand settled on her forearm. Warmth cut through the cold that had clamped around her ribs. "Hey," he said, voice low, meant only for her. "You alright?"

Maret watched the door at the top of the stairs. The wood grain. The iron hinge. The stillness of it. She swallowed. Her throat felt lined with dust.

"That was the Arbiter," she said. The words came out flat. A statement of recorded fact.

"I gathered." Theo didn't remove his hand. His thumb rested just above her wrist, steady. "You're white as chalk, Maret. Your knuckles are still gripping that post like you're about to tear it out of the floor."

She looked down. Her fingers were pale, the tendons standing sharp beneath the skin. She loosened them one at a time.

"A scribe's duties are administrative," she said. The phrase tasted hollow.

"Sure." Theo's eyes held hers, unblinking. "And I'm an adventurer with a Strength of nine."

She blinked.

"The point is," he said, softer now, "you don't have to tell me what that was about. But if he comes back, or if you need someone to stand between you and whatever ledger he's trying to balance—" He squeezed her arm, just once. "I can watch your back. Keep my mouth shut. Sign my name to anything you need witnessed."

The hearth-fire crackled. A log shifted in the grate, sending a spray of sparks up the chimney. Maret stared at the grain of the post, at the place where her forehead had been pressed, at the faint damp mark her skin had left on the wood.

The mark was almost gone now. In a few more breaths, no one would ever know she had pressed her face against the wood like a woman bracing for a storm.

"Witness," she repeated. The word tasted strange. "You don't even know what you'd be witnessing."

Theo smiled, but it didn't reach his eyes. "That's the point, isn't it? You don't want me to know. So I won't ask. But if someone needs to verify that you were here, at a certain hour, doing nothing suspicious—I can do that." He spread his hands. "I'm a journeyman adventurer. My word carries weight with the Guild."

Maret's throat tightened. She could feel the shape of it—the offer, genuine and open-handed. A shield without questions. A friend without conditions. Eight years of isolation had taught her to refuse such things on instinct, to assume every hand extended carried a chain she couldn't see.

But Theo's hands were empty.

"The Guild doesn't care about scribes," she said. "No one does. That's the point."

"I care."

The words landed like a stone in still water. Maret watched the ripples spread across his face—the slight furrow of his brow, the way his jaw set with quiet stubbornness. He meant it. Of course he meant it. That was the problem.

"You shouldn't." She pulled her sleeve straight, smoothed the fabric over her wrist. "The Arbiter is not a man you want to be on the wrong side of."

"And you are?"

The question hung in the air between them. Maret opened her mouth, closed it. The tavern's noise swelled—a burst of laughter from the bar, the scrape of a chair being dragged across floorboards. Someone called for another round.

"I'm a scribe," she said finally. The words felt thin, inadequate. "I copy documents. I file requests. I—"

"You saved my life."

"In a library. With a bookshelf."

"And that's all?" Theo's voice was gentle, but it pressed anyway. "A Strength 8 scribe with a lucky shove?"

Maret's fingers found the edge of her robe again, rolled the threadbare fabric between them. She could feel the whole structure of her lie trembling, every joist and beam she had spent eight years constructing, and Theo was standing in the middle of it, asking her to let him help hold it up.

"You don't know what you're asking," she said.

"I'm not asking anything." He stepped closer, close enough that she could smell the hearth-smoke in his clothes. "I'm offering. There's a difference."

The difference, she thought, was that an offer could be refused. And she should refuse it. She should tell him to walk away, to pretend they had never met, to forget everything he had seen and heard tonight.

"Thank you," she said. The words scraped coming out. "For the offer. But a scribe's duties are administrative. There's nothing to witness."

Theo held her gaze for a long moment. Then he nodded, slow and deliberate, and took a step back.

"Alright, Maret." He picked up his tankard from the table, drained the last of the ale. "But the offer stands. Any time."

He walked toward the door, pausing at the threshold to glance back. The firelight caught his profile, quick and warm. Then he was gone, and the tavern's noise rushed in to fill the space he had left.

Maret stood alone at the post, her hand still pressed to her sleeve. The damp mark on the wood had vanished entirely, leaving no trace.

*She could do the same,* she thought. Leave. Vanish into the night, find another town, another guild hall, another stack of documents to lose herself in.

The hearth-fire cracked and settled. The warmth touched her face, and she did not move toward the door.

The hearth-fire cracked and settled, a low orange pulse against the stone. Maret watched the flames fold inward, then rise again.

Vanish into the night — the thought had teeth. Another town. Another guild hall. Another stack of documents to lose herself in. The warmth touched her face, and she did not move toward the door.

The summons lay where Theo had set it, its seal already broken. The ink was dry, the script official. *Interrogation to begin by noon.* Twelve hours. Perhaps less, if the guard chose to collect her early.

A scribe would not run. Running implied guilt. Guilt invited questions a scribe could not answer.

She pressed her palms flat against the table's scored surface. The city guard had been instructed to watch the Scribal Quarter's exits — Theo had said as much, though he'd framed it as routine. Routine watch meant routine records. Her name would appear in the log. Vanishing into the night would become an entry in a ledger — one that any senior scribe would know how to read.

She could not outrun the paper trail.

A slow breath. The hearth-fire popped. Maret reached into her satchel and withdrew her personal log — the one she kept separate from the guild's official records. The spine was worn, the pages soft with use. Inside were numbers that did not match the System's public profile. Strength values that had no business on a scribe's sheet.

She could hide this log. Burn it. Leave a doctored copy in plain sight.

Her fingers traced the edge of the cover. To destroy the record was to destroy eight years of careful observation. But to keep it was to leave a weapon at the interrogator's feet.

The hearth-fire waited. The summons waited. The clock in the square struck a quarter hour.

Maret tucked the log back into her satchel, then pulled out a fresh sheaf of blank parchment. She would spend the night preparing — a true log altered enough to deflect suspicion, detailed enough to satisfy. A scribe's strength was in her quill. Tonight, that would have to be enough.

The flames cast her shadow long against the wall. She set the summons beside the blank page, squared her shoulders, and began to write.

You are a prose writer. Your job is to write one scene beat of a larger chapter based on the beat description and context provided.

Respond with ONLY the prose text. No JSON, no wrapper, no commentary. Just the scene.

Rules:
- Dramatize this beat. The beat description is your creative brief — capture its essential dramatic content through scene, dialogue, and character action. You decide how to show it.
- Write approximately the target word count. Do not summarize or abbreviate.
- Structure the beat as: GOAL → CONFLICT → DISASTER (or success at a cost). If this beat ends a sequence, include a brief sequel: reaction → decision that connects to the next beat.
- Speech pattern is law: each character's speechPattern defines how they talk. Do not deviate.
- The POV character's vocabulary and worldview color the narration.
- Show emotion through body and action. Never name emotions in narration.
- Every beat with 2+ characters needs spoken dialogue. Characters talk around the real issue — subtext, not exposition.
- Anchor paragraphs in sensory detail specific to the setting.
- Use \n\n between paragraphs.
- If a TRANSITION BRIDGE is provided, continue naturally from where it left off. NEVER repeat or echo dialogue, phrases, or imagery from the bridge — it is already written. Move the scene forward.
- If a LANDING TARGET is provided, end on a moment that connects toward it.
- Each beat must introduce NEW action, dialogue, and detail. Do not recycle lines or motifs from previous beats.

response.content:
The summons sat in her sleeve, a crisp spine against her wrist. Maret kept her eyes on the table’s scarred surface, her thumb tracing the paper’s edge through the fabric.

“There is a commission due before dawn,” she said. “Sixty-seven pages requiring attestation. Seals, manifests, route charters for the northern trade routes. The merchant convoy loses its departure window if I do not complete it.”

Lucius Cassel remained standing. His hands were clasped behind his back, his coat unbuttoned, his posture that of a man reading from a legal docket he had long since committed to memory. “Per Article Twelve of the Veridith Accord, Arbiter authority supersedes guild obligation. The Scribal Guild may file a protest with the Quartermaster. That process requires a minimum of fourteen days to adjudicate.”

“The Guild Master will not let this pass without — ”

“The Guild Master may address his concerns to the Precinct directly. That is his right under Section Four of the Accord.” Cassel’s voice did not rise. It did not need to. “The timetable is not negotiable, Scribe Halsey. Noon tomorrow. You will present yourself at the Arbiter Precinct, ground floor, chamber three.”

Noon. Not three mornings. Not enough time to disappear, to think, to breathe.

The tavern’s murmur thinned around them. A spoon clinked against a bowl. The old woman by the hearth had stopped chewing. Behind the bar, the tavernkeep’s hand hovered above a tankard he had been polishing, the cloth frozen mid-stroke.

Maret kept her breathing even. “The Framework records every summons. If the Guild Master discovers I was pulled from a binding commission without due notice, the political cost to your office — ”

“Is accounted for.” Cassel reached into his coat and produced a folded document, sealed in grey wax. He set it on the table between them, his fingers releasing it with deliberate care. “This is your formal summons. The date, time, location, and presiding Arbiter are recorded in full. You will sign the acknowledgment at the bottom.”

She did not reach for it.

“And if I refuse to sign?”

Cassel’s gaze did not waver. “Then I will record your verbal acknowledgment, which carries the same weight per the Evidentiary Standards Act. But I feel I should make the consequence clear, Scribe Halsey.”

He paused. The silence in the room was absolute.

“If you fail to appear at noon tomorrow, or if you attempt to absent yourself from Veridith before that time, I will escalate the matter to a warrant for arrest. All gate guards and city patrols will be notified before the hour following your absence is complete. Veridith has two thousand three hundred registered citizens confined within its walls tonight. You will not slip through a blind spot.”

Maret’s fingers tightened around the paper in her sleeve. The crease bit into her thumb.

She reached out, took the summons from the table, and read the date. *Noon. Chamber three. The seventh of Emberleaf.*

“I understand the terms, Arbiter Cassel.”

She did not say she would comply. Only that she understood.

request.systemPrompt:
You verify that chapter prose captures the INTENT of a chapter plan. Beat descriptions are creative inspiration, NOT literal scripts.

Compare the CHAPTER PROSE against the CHAPTER PLAN and fill out a structured checklist. You MUST fill out every field before reaching a verdict. Do not skip any field.

For each check, write down what you actually observed in the prose. Then reach a verdict based on your own observations.

CHECKS TO FILL OUT:

1. **setting_match** — Compare the plan's setting to where the prose actually takes place.
   - planned: copy the setting field from the plan
   - observed: quote a phrase from the prose that establishes the location
   - matches: true if the observed location is the same place as planned (minor spatial variation is fine — different room in the same building is a match). false if the prose is set in a completely different location. If the prose transitions between locations across beats, matches=true as long as the primary setting appears.

2. **emotional_arc_correct** — Does the prose match the overall emotional direction of the plan's final beat? true if the ending emotion is in the same direction as planned (e.g., both resolve to anger, both resolve to relief). false ONLY if the direction is REVERSED (a tension-escalating beat resolved it instead, or vice versa).

3. **pass** — PASS unless:
   - setting_match is false, OR
   - emotional_arc_correct is false, OR
   - the prose introduces a major plot contradiction (e.g., a character dies when the plan has them alive later, a resolved conflict is re-opened without cause, a character knows something they shouldn't yet)

4. **deviations** — list every specific problem you identified. Empty list if pass=true.
   Each deviation MUST have:
   - `description` — the specific problem in plain English
   - `beat_index` — the 0-indexed beat number in the plan's beats[] array that the problem refers to. Use `null` ONLY when the problem is chapter-level and cannot be attributed to a specific beat (e.g. setting mismatch spanning the whole chapter, or an emotional arc that drifts across many beats).

DO NOT flag these as deviations — they are normal creative interpretation:
- Paraphrased dialogue
- Reordered details within a beat
- Added atmospheric details, props, or sensory descriptions
- Slightly different physical actions that serve the same narrative purpose
- Minor spatial variations (sitting vs standing, different part of the room)
- Missing individual beat events
- Characters absent from a single beat

Respond with ONLY valid JSON in this exact shape:
{
  "setting_match": { "planned": "...", "observed": "...", "matches": true },
  "emotional_arc_correct": true,
  "pass": true,
  "deviations": [
    { "description": "Taryn refuses the offer, but plan's beat 10 requires acceptance", "beat_index": 10 }
  ]
}

CHAPTER PROSE:
The Pious Rest hummed with the comfort of evening trade. A fire crackled in the great hearth, casting orange light across scarred oak tables where scribes hunched over cups of mulled wine and adventurers argued the price of cured deep-glow hides. The air carried the sharp bite of roasted meat, the yeasty breath of fresh ale, and the faint sourness of sawdust ground into the floorboards by countless boots.

Maret sat with her back to the wall, a bowl of brown stew cooling between her hands. Across the table, Theo tore a heel of dark bread in half and offered one piece to her.

"The guild's latest commission," she said, accepting the bread with a nod. "The estimate lists seventeen copper-leaf specimens requiring preservation from the archive transfer. Someone clearly failed to seal their sample satchels properly on the last expedition."

Theo grinned around a mouthful of stew. "You say that like you're not secretly thrilled. Seventeen specimens means seventeen entries in the master index. That's a whole page to yourself."

"A page that no one reads." She dipped her bread into the broth. "The record's purpose is to exist, not to be admired."

"That's the most scribe thing I've ever heard you say." He washed down the bite with a pull from his tankard. "You ever think about takin' up somethin' with a bit more... movement? Not combat, obviously. But maybe survey work? Out in the field instead of in the archive?"

Maret's spoon paused halfway to her mouth. "Survey work requires a Pathfinder attunement or a minimum Agility of 14. The record shows scribes are ill-suited for field placement beyond static logistical support."

"Right. Of course." Theo's tone was light, but his eyes lingered on her a moment too long before he looked away. "Just thinkin' out loud."

The lute player in the corner shifted into a faster reel. Laughter erupted from a table of merchants near the bar, and for a moment the tavern felt full and safe and ordinary.

Then the front door scraped open.

The door swung shut behind him with a soft click that cut through the din. The lute player faltered for half a measure, then recovered.

The man in the grey coat walked straight toward their table, boots silent on the worn floorboards. He didn't scan the room, didn't glance at the bar. His eyes were fixed on Maret, and they did not waver.

He stopped a foot from their table, one hand resting on the leather folder tucked under his arm. The candlelight caught the silver brooch at his collar—a gavel crossed with a quill, the Arbiter's seal.

"Scribe Maret Halsey," he said. The words were precise, each one clipped clean. "I am Arbiter Lucius Cassel, dispatched per protocol to review a discrepancy logged in your class records."

Theo's chair scraped back. "Hey, hold on—"

But Cassel was already pulling out the empty chair across from Maret and settling into it, setting the folder flat on the table between them. He did not ask.

"Your cooperation is not required, Journeyman Marlow," Cassel said without looking at him. "But it would be efficient."

Maret's hand moved to her tankard, fingers wrapping around the cool ceramic. *A scribe's strength is in her quill.* She felt her pulse in her throat.

"Greetings, Arbiter." Her voice came out steady. "A discrepancy. That seems unlikely. I've kept my records in order."

Cassel's mouth twitched—not quite a smile. "The System's logs are the matter, not your own." He placed his palm on the folder. "And they are very clear."

Maret's fingers rested on the edge of her tankard. The clay was warm, porous. She could crush it without effort. She did not.

"Then the logs have misread something." Her voice stayed flat, administrative. "A scribe's duties are administrative. The incident involved a falling shelf and quick reflexes. There is nothing to explain."

Lucius Cassel's mouth remained perfectly still. He flipped the folder open with one precise motion. Inside lay a single sheet, cream parchment bordered by the embossed Arbiter seal—a hollow circle stamped in black wax, the ink catching the hearthlight like wet iron. He slid it across the table. "The seal renders it valid for enforcement."

She did not touch the document. She read it upside down, scanning the formal petition for attendance, the case number, the section of the Framework cited. Her name. Her class. Her assigned precinct. The date—three mornings from now.

Her throat tightened. "An interrogation."

"Formal clarification." Cassel's hands rested on either side of the folder, palms flat. "You will be asked to explain the divergence between your recorded attributes and the observed instance of—" He paused, choosing the words carefully. "—physical capacity displayed during the incident at the Guild Repository."

"The shelf fell. People react. The System reads what it reads."

"The logs indicate otherwise, Scribe Maret." His voice remained level, but something beneath it had hardened. "Per the internal record, your Strength attribute was logged at three at the time of the event. That is a fixed value. Immutable. Yet the shelf in question—a wrought-iron structure weighing approximately forty pounds—was bent at a thirty-degree angle across its left support arm."

She said nothing.

"Forty pounds of iron does not bend at thirty degrees from a falling impact. The angle suggests deliberate force applied from below. Upward. Through the center span." He tapped the document once. "The Arbiter Precinct requires your attendance to reconcile this discrepancy."

Maret kept her hands still. The tankard sat untouched. Across the room, the fire cracked and a drunk laughed at something said in jest. The sounds belonged to a different world than the one inside her chest.

"Three days," she said.

"Three mornings. You will present yourself at the Precinct foyer by the seventh bell. An examiner will be assigned to record your testimony." Cassel straightened the folder against the table's edge. "Failure to appear constitutes obstruction under Section Twelve of the Framework. The penalty is reclassification."

Reclassification. The word sat between them like a blade laid on the cloth. A scribe reclassified was a scribe unmade—reassigned to manual labor at the outskirts, stripped of guild protections, erased from the rolls.

She reached for the document. Her fingers brushed the embossed seal. "I will be there."

Cassel nodded once, a small, mechanical motion. He closed the folder and tucked it beneath his arm. "The Arbiter Precinct thanks you for your cooperation, Scribe Maret."

He turned. His boots made soft sounds against the worn floorboards as he walked toward the door.

From the bar, Theo's voice cut through the tavern noise. "Hold on. What kind of discrepancies are we talking about? And why would an Arbiter handle a scribe's paperwork?"

Lucius Cassel’s gaze shifted to Theo with the measured economy of a man who counted every motion. His fingers remained still on the tabletop.

“Journeyman Marlow.” No warmth. No greeting. “The discrepancies pertain to recorded attribute values that do not align with observed physical evidence. Specifically, a logged Strength of three and a bent iron lantern hook requiring a minimum effective Strength of twelve to deform. As to your second question: an Arbiter handles any matter where the System’s records may have been compromised, regardless of the class involved. This falls under Section Eight of the Framework Code—anomalies in class attribution.”

He let the words settle. The tavern hearth crackled. A mug thudded against oak somewhere behind Maret’s shoulder.

“I should also note,” Cassel continued, his voice dropping to a register that barely carried past their table, “that obstruction of an Arbiter’s duty carries separate penalties under Section Fourteen. Your involvement in this matter is not requested.”

Theo’s jaw tightened. He did not look away. “Just askin’ questions. That’s not obstruction. I’ve got a right to be curious when an Arbiter walks into my regular tavern and starts talkin’ about bent iron.”

“Curiosity becomes obstruction the moment it impedes a clean investigation.” Cassel’s tone did not rise. It did not need to. “I advise you to return to your companions, Journeyman. Your party remains registered at the Adventurers’ Guild. Your next delve is scheduled for three days hence. You have preparations to attend to.”

Maret watched Theo’s fingers curl around the edge of the table. Knuckles pale. He wanted to push. She could see it in the set of his shoulders, the way his jaw worked against words he hadn’t spoken yet.

He glanced at her.

She gave a tiny shake of her head. Barely a motion. *Not now. Not here.*

Theo held her gaze for a beat longer than was comfortable, then let out a breath through his nose. “Fine.” He pushed back his chair, the legs scraping against wide oak floorboards. He did not look at Cassel again. “Maret. I’ll be at the bar if you need me.”

He left.

Cassel did not watch him go. Instead, his hand moved to the leather satchel at his side, opened the brass clasp with a soft click, and withdrew a single sheet of parchment. He placed it flat on the table between them, turned so the words faced Maret.

“This is the official summons,” he said. “Per protocol, you are to present yourself at the Arbiter Precinct for a formal review of the discrepancy. The time and date are recorded.”

The seal at the bottom glinted—a silver embossment of the Framework’s interlocking rings. Maret’s eyes traced the lines of text, her throat dry, her fingers still pressed flat against her own thigh beneath the table.

The words blurred once, then settled. Maret read them again, this time letting her eyes linger on each syllable.

*Noon. Tomorrow. Arbiter Precinct.*

She committed it to memory—the curl of the *n* in *noon*, the sharp downward stroke of the *p* in *Precinct*. A scribe's habit. Useful when the hands shook and the mind needed something solid to hold.

The next sentence she already knew. She had transcribed enough summonses to recognize the formula. *Failure to appear before the appointed Arbiter shall constitute de jure admission of culpability.*

No exceptions. No delays written into the text.

Her fingertip traced the embossed rings a second time. Cold. Precise. The Framework's mark of authority pressed into the fiber of the page, permanent as a scar.

She set the parchment down and watched it settle against the grain of the table. Outside the tavern's common room, a chair scraped stone. Someone called for another round. The sounds reached her from a great distance, as though she were underwater.

Eight years. Eight years of logging unremarkable stats, taking unremarkable commissions, living an unremarkable life in a room above a baker's shop with a window that faced an alley and never, ever drew attention. And now this.

The summons didn't accuse. It didn't need to. It was a procedural invitation—that was the fiction they maintained. *Review of discrepancy.* A neutral phrase. A polite clearing of the throat.

She read the line a third time. *Formal review.* There was nothing neutral about that.

Her hand moved to the edge of the table. The wood was warm where the candle had sat. She pressed her palm flat.

There was the guild commission. The lineage charter—three generations of births, marriages, and deaths to verify against the municipal register. Due by week's end. Critical, the guildmaster had said. The family needed it sealed before the inheritance hearing.

If she cited guild protocol, if she demanded a delay on grounds of an active contractual obligation, she could buy herself time. A day. Maybe two.

She could use that time to run.

Or she could use it to prepare.

Maret picked up the summons, folded it once along its spine, and slid it into her sleeve without looking away from the table.

The summons sat in her sleeve, a crisp spine against her wrist. Maret kept her eyes on the table’s scarred surface, her thumb tracing the paper’s edge through the fabric.

“There is a commission due before dawn,” she said. “Sixty-seven pages requiring attestation. Seals, manifests, route charters for the northern trade routes. The merchant convoy loses its departure window if I do not complete it.”

Lucius Cassel remained standing. His hands were clasped behind his back, his coat unbuttoned, his posture that of a man reading from a legal docket he had long since committed to memory. “Per Article Twelve of the Veridith Accord, Arbiter authority supersedes guild obligation. The Scribal Guild may file a protest with the Quartermaster. That process requires a minimum of fourteen days to adjudicate.”

“The Guild Master will not let this pass without — ”

“The Guild Master may address his concerns to the Precinct directly. That is his right under Section Four of the Accord.” Cassel’s voice did not rise. It did not need to. “The timetable is not negotiable, Scribe Halsey. Noon tomorrow. You will present yourself at the Arbiter Precinct, ground floor, chamber three.”

Noon. Not three mornings. Not enough time to disappear, to think, to breathe.

The tavern’s murmur thinned around them. A spoon clinked against a bowl. The old woman by the hearth had stopped chewing. Behind the bar, the tavernkeep’s hand hovered above a tankard he had been polishing, the cloth frozen mid-stroke.

Maret kept her breathing even. “The Framework records every summons. If the Guild Master discovers I was pulled from a binding commission without due notice, the political cost to your office — ”

“Is accounted for.” Cassel reached into his coat and produced a folded document, sealed in grey wax. He set it on the table between them, his fingers releasing it with deliberate care. “This is your formal summons. The date, time, location, and presiding Arbiter are recorded in full. You will sign the acknowledgment at the bottom.”

She did not reach for it.

“And if I refuse to sign?”

Cassel’s gaze did not waver. “Then I will record your verbal acknowledgment, which carries the same weight per the Evidentiary Standards Act. But I feel I should make the consequence clear, Scribe Halsey.”

He paused. The silence in the room was absolute.

“If you fail to appear at noon tomorrow, or if you attempt to absent yourself from Veridith before that time, I will escalate the matter to a warrant for arrest. All gate guards and city patrols will be notified before the hour following your absence is complete. Veridith has two thousand three hundred registered citizens confined within its walls tonight. You will not slip through a blind spot.”

Maret’s fingers tightened around the paper in her sleeve. The crease bit into her thumb.

She reached out, took the summons from the table, and read the date. *Noon. Chamber three. The seventh of Emberleaf.*

“I understand the terms, Arbiter Cassel.”

She did not say she would comply. Only that she understood.

She pressed her thumb against its crease. “I understand the terms, Arbiter Cassel.”

She did not say she would comply. Only that she understood.

Arbiter Cassel did not move to take the paper back. He let it sit between them, a dead thing on the stained oak. The tavern’s fire popped and settled, and somewhere behind the bar a glass clinked against a bottle. The air smelled of old ale and beeswax.

“Then you also understand the consequence of refusal,” he said. His voice had no edge — it was flat, procedural, as if he were reading from a statute. “If you fail to appear before the System tribunal by dawn, I will file a writ of compulsion. The warrant will be circulated to every city watch post in Veridith. Your description, your class, your last known location. Per the Framework’s emergency protocols, a fugitive marked as an anomaly triggers priority capture. No exceptions.”

Maret kept her hands still on the tabletop. The scarred wood had a long groove running through the grain, the memory of a knife drawn deep and hard. She traced its path with her thumb as she spoke.

“The watch has little interest in scribes.”

“They will have interest in a scribe who bends iron and fails to report.” He leaned back, just slightly, a motion that barely shifted the candle flame between them. “I do not say this to threaten, Scribe Maret. I say it because the System does not tolerate contradictions. If you flee, you prove the anomaly’s danger. You prove that your silence was deliberate. You prove that you are a threat to order.”

She heard the word he did not speak. It hung in the air between them, unsaid but unmistakable.

The fire crackled. A patron laughed too loudly at the bar. The sound felt distant, as if it came from behind a wall of glass.

Lucius Cassel stood. He was taller than her by half a head, and the candlelight carved shadows into the planes of his face. He left the paper on the table.

“You have until dawn.” He turned, his boots heavy on the floorboards, and walked toward the door.

Maret did not watch him leave. She looked at her hands on the scarred wood. At the document lying between them like a sealed fate. The lamp oil had begun to pool in its shallow dish, burning low.

She had known, in the abstract, that discovery meant a choice. But the abstract had never had weight before. It had never tasted of soot and tallow and the end of things.

Her thumb found the groove in the wood again. A path carved by someone else’s blade, someone else’s trouble, years before she ever set foot in this tavern.

She traced it once.

Then she stopped.

The fire in the common room had burned low, orange embers pulsing like a heartbeat beneath a blanket of ash. Maret sat alone at the corner table, her untouched cup of tea gone cold. The tavern's last patrons had drifted away an hour ago—a pair of tired merchants, a drunk who'd fallen asleep over his stew—and now the only sounds were the distant clatter of pots from the kitchen and the soft creak of the innkeeper's chair.

She traced the rim of the cup with a fingertip. The path she'd stopped tracing years ago had begun to glow again, brighter than she'd ever wanted to see it.

*Compliance.* The word sat in her chest like a stone. Walk into the Guild Assessment Hall, present her character sheet, let the etching-class readers parse her stats. Wait for the moment when their eyes would snag on the discrepancy—the +4 Strength that made her a contradiction, a living clerical error. They would call it an anomaly. They would record it. They would summon the guild's special assessors from the capital, the ones who studied System defects like specimens pinned to a board. Maret Halsey, once filed under "Journeyman Scribe," would become a case file. A research subject. A thing to be understood.

She had seen their reports in the guild archives. Thin, precise handwriting. Conclusions that left no room for argument.

*Flight.* The other fork. A bag thrown together in the dark, the back door of the tavern, the road south through the wilds—a stretch of untracked forest and scrubland that swallowed travelers whole when they weren't looking. No papers, no register, no trace. She could vanish into the frontier settlements, work for farmers who paid in bread and never asked for a class. She would become a ghost. No more guild dues, no more quiet evenings in the scriptorium with the smell of ink and old paper. No more Theo, who brought her pastries from the market and asked too many questions with his earnest, freckled face.

Her chest tightened at that last thought. She pressed a palm flat against the table's scarred surface until the grain bit into her skin. Eight years of careful silence, of building a life so small and ordinary that no one looked twice. Eight years of pretending the Strength stat on her sheet was a scribe's ink-smudge, a glitch, a nothing.

But she knew the truth. The +4 was real. It had always been real. And the System had given it to her for a reason she still did not understand.

The innkeeper's chair creaked again. A log shifted in the hearth, sending a brief flare of light across the room. Maret watched the shadows leap and settle.

*Compliance meant certainty:* her secret pried open, her body measured, her future written by someone else's hand. *Flight meant uncertainty:* hunger, cold, the constant weight of looking over her shoulder. But uncertainty also meant *choice*. She could still choose the direction of her own feet.

Except Theo would wake tomorrow and find her gone. No note. No explanation. He would ask Cassel, and Cassel would shrug, and eventually someone would say *she probably ran* and he would have to decide whether to believe it.

Maret picked up the cold cup of tea and set it down again without drinking.

She needed time. Time to think, to plan, to decide whether the cost of flight was higher than the cost of staying. One night. That was all she was asking for—one night to arrange her affairs, to close her ledgers, to look Theo in the eye one last time without knowing whether it was goodbye.

She rose from the table and turned toward the stairs where Cassel's room lay.

She stopped at the base of the stairs, one hand resting on the worn newel post. The wood was smooth from decades of gripping. She turned back to where Lucius Cassel still sat at the corner table, his hands flat on either side of the leather folder.

"Arbiter Cassel." Her voice carried across the near-empty tavern. A single lamp flickered on the bar, throwing long shadows across the floorboards. "I need until the morning."

Cassel did not move. The candle at his table burned steady, unmoved by any draft. "Define the term, Scribe Maret."

"To arrange my guild affairs." She kept her hand on the post—steady, patient. The wood held her weight without a creak. "Ledgers to close. Standing orders to transfer. A journeyman to inform." She paused. "One night."

He opened the folder. His eyes traced a line of text, then another. The silence stretched. Maret counted her breaths—three, four, five—before he closed it again with a precise snap.

"Until noon. Not a bell more." His voice carried no warmth, no sympathy. "The interrogation must begin by the twelfth hour. Per protocol, an absentee subject may be classified as a fugitive. I will not grant extensions."

She nodded once. "Noon."

"There is a further matter." He slid a hand into his coat and produced a folded slip of parchment. He did not offer it to her. "The city guard has been instructed to watch all exits from the Scribal Quarter. As of dusk, no one departs without recorded timestamps and destination. This is not negotiable."

Maret's fingers tightened on the newel post, then relaxed. "I understand."

Cassel rose. He collected the folder, tucked the parchment back into his coat, and straightened his collar. His eyes met hers for a moment—flat, assessing, revealing nothing. Then he turned and walked toward the back staircase without another word.

The door at the top of the stairs clicked shut. The candle flame trembled, then steadied.

Maret let out a long, slow breath and pressed her forehead against the cool wood of the post.

Cassel had already turned and walked away before the door clicked shut. The sound hung in the air—sharp, final, the period at the end of a sentence she hadn't wanted to read.

The tavern noise rushed back in like water filling a footprint. A mug clattered. Someone laughed. A chair groaned under shifting weight.

Theo's hand settled on her forearm. Warmth cut through the cold that had clamped around her ribs. "Hey," he said, voice low, meant only for her. "You alright?"

Maret watched the door at the top of the stairs. The wood grain. The iron hinge. The stillness of it. She swallowed. Her throat felt lined with dust.

"That was the Arbiter," she said. The words came out flat. A statement of recorded fact.

"I gathered." Theo didn't remove his hand. His thumb rested just above her wrist, steady. "You're white as chalk, Maret. Your knuckles are still gripping that post like you're about to tear it out of the floor."

She looked down. Her fingers were pale, the tendons standing sharp beneath the skin. She loosened them one at a time.

"A scribe's duties are administrative," she said. The phrase tasted hollow.

"Sure." Theo's eyes held hers, unblinking. "And I'm an adventurer with a Strength of nine."

She blinked.

"The point is," he said, softer now, "you don't have to tell me what that was about. But if he comes back, or if you need someone to stand between you and whatever ledger he's trying to balance—" He squeezed her arm, just once. "I can watch your back. Keep my mouth shut. Sign my name to anything you need witnessed."

The hearth-fire crackled. A log shifted in the grate, sending a spray of sparks up the chimney. Maret stared at the grain of the post, at the place where her forehead had been pressed, at the faint damp mark her skin had left on the wood.

The mark was almost gone now. In a few more breaths, no one would ever know she had pressed her face against the wood like a woman bracing for a storm.

"Witness," she repeated. The word tasted strange. "You don't even know what you'd be witnessing."

Theo smiled, but it didn't reach his eyes. "That's the point, isn't it? You don't want me to know. So I won't ask. But if someone needs to verify that you were here, at a certain hour, doing nothing suspicious—I can do that." He spread his hands. "I'm a journeyman adventurer. My word carries weight with the Guild."

Maret's throat tightened. She could feel the shape of it—the offer, genuine and open-handed. A shield without questions. A friend without conditions. Eight years of isolation had taught her to refuse such things on instinct, to assume every hand extended carried a chain she couldn't see.

But Theo's hands were empty.

"The Guild doesn't care about scribes," she said. "No one does. That's the point."

"I care."

The words landed like a stone in still water. Maret watched the ripples spread across his face—the slight furrow of his brow, the way his jaw set with quiet stubbornness. He meant it. Of course he meant it. That was the problem.

"You shouldn't." She pulled her sleeve straight, smoothed the fabric over her wrist. "The Arbiter is not a man you want to be on the wrong side of."

"And you are?"

The question hung in the air between them. Maret opened her mouth, closed it. The tavern's noise swelled—a burst of laughter from the bar, the scrape of a chair being dragged across floorboards. Someone called for another round.

"I'm a scribe," she said finally. The words felt thin, inadequate. "I copy documents. I file requests. I—"

"You saved my life."

"In a library. With a bookshelf."

"And that's all?" Theo's voice was gentle, but it pressed anyway. "A Strength 8 scribe with a lucky shove?"

Maret's fingers found the edge of her robe again, rolled the threadbare fabric between them. She could feel the whole structure of her lie trembling, every joist and beam she had spent eight years constructing, and Theo was standing in the middle of it, asking her to let him help hold it up.

"You don't know what you're asking," she said.

"I'm not asking anything." He stepped closer, close enough that she could smell the hearth-smoke in his clothes. "I'm offering. There's a difference."

The difference, she thought, was that an offer could be refused. And she should refuse it. She should tell him to walk away, to pretend they had never met, to forget everything he had seen and heard tonight.

"Thank you," she said. The words scraped coming out. "For the offer. But a scribe's duties are administrative. There's nothing to witness."

Theo held her gaze for a long moment. Then he nodded, slow and deliberate, and took a step back.

"Alright, Maret." He picked up his tankard from the table, drained the last of the ale. "But the offer stands. Any time."

He walked toward the door, pausing at the threshold to glance back. The firelight caught his profile, quick and warm. Then he was gone, and the tavern's noise rushed in to fill the space he had left.

Maret stood alone at the post, her hand still pressed to her sleeve. The damp mark on the wood had vanished entirely, leaving no trace.

*She could do the same,* she thought. Leave. Vanish into the night, find another town, another guild hall, another stack of documents to lose herself in.

The hearth-fire cracked and settled. The warmth touched her face, and she did not move toward the door.

The hearth-fire cracked and settled, a low orange pulse against the stone. Maret watched the flames fold inward, then rise again.

Vanish into the night — the thought had teeth. Another town. Another guild hall. Another stack of documents to lose herself in. The warmth touched her face, and she did not move toward the door.

The summons lay where Theo had set it, its seal already broken. The ink was dry, the script official. *Interrogation to begin by noon.* Twelve hours. Perhaps less, if the guard chose to collect her early.

A scribe would not run. Running implied guilt. Guilt invited questions a scribe could not answer.

She pressed her palms flat against the table's scored surface. The city guard had been instructed to watch the Scribal Quarter's exits — Theo had said as much, though he'd framed it as routine. Routine watch meant routine records. Her name would appear in the log. Vanishing into the night would become an entry in a ledger — one that any senior scribe would know how to read.

She could not outrun the paper trail.

A slow breath. The hearth-fire popped. Maret reached into her satchel and withdrew her personal log — the one she kept separate from the guild's official records. The spine was worn, the pages soft with use. Inside were numbers that did not match the System's public profile. Strength values that had no business on a scribe's sheet.

She could hide this log. Burn it. Leave a doctored copy in plain sight.

Her fingers traced the edge of the cover. To destroy the record was to destroy eight years of careful observation. But to keep it was to leave a weapon at the interrogator's feet.

The hearth-fire waited. The summons waited. The clock in the square struck a quarter hour.

Maret tucked the log back into her satchel, then pulled out a fresh sheaf of blank parchment. She would spend the night preparing — a true log altered enough to deflect suspicion, detailed enough to satisfy. A scribe's strength was in her quill. Tonight, that would have to be enough.

The flames cast her shadow long against the wall. She set the summons beside the blank page, squared her shoulders, and began to write.
